chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-1. Purpose
chunks.text:
This regulation prescribes policies, procedures, and responsibilities for developing, managing, and con-
ducting Army training and leader development.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-2. References, forms, and explanation of abbreviations
chunks.text:
See appendix A. The abbreviations, brevity codes, and acronyms (ABCAs) used in this electronic publica-
tion are defined when you hover over them. All ABCAs are listed in the ABCA directory located at
https://armypubs.army.mil/.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-3. Associated publications
chunks.text: This section contains no entries.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-4. Responsibilities
chunks.text: Responsibilities are listed in chapter 2.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-5. Records management (recordkeeping) requirements
chunks.text:
The records management requirement for all record numbers, associated forms, and reports required by
this publication are addressed in the Records Retention Schedule-Army (RRS-A). Detailed information
for all related record numbers, forms, and reports are located in Army Records Information Management
System (ARIMS)/RRS-A at https://www.arims.army.mil. If any record numbers, forms, and reports are not
current, addressed, and/or published correctly in ARIMS/RRS-A, see DA Pam 25-403 for guidance.
Section II
Army Mission and Sustaining Training Readiness

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-6. Army priorities affecting training and leader development:
chunks.text:
Warfighting Readiness. The Army remains ready to deploy, fight, and win our Nation’s wars as part
of the joint force. This means prioritizing training, fitness, discipline, and lethality at all levels. We will culti-
vate a warrior mindset in our Soldiers and Army Civilians, ensuring they are mentally and physically pre-
pared for the rigors of combat. This also involves rigorously training as we fight, leveraging the lessons of
history and modern warfare to develop the most lethal and effective fighting force possible.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-6. Army priorities affecting training and leader development:
chunks.text:
Transformation. To maintain our edge, the Army conducts continuous and deliberate transfor-
mation. This includes rapidly integrating cutting-edge technology, optimizing procurement processes to
equip our forces swiftly, and fostering a culture of innovation where warfighters are empowered to find so-
lutions. Streamlining our systems and embracing new technologies will enable the Army to fight and win
on future battlefields.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-6. Army priorities affecting training and leader development:
chunks.text:
Strengthening the Army Profession. The bedrock of our success lies in the strength and resilience
of the Army profession. This involves fostering an unwavering sense of purpose and shared identity
among our Soldiers and Army Civilians. This commitment will revitalize national pride in the Army, bolster
recruitment, and reaffirm that our Soldiers and their families remain our most vital asset.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-7. Training Readiness Modernization
chunks.text:
The Army updated its force generation model to a Regionally Aligned Readiness and Modernization
Model (ReARMM) to better balance the life cycle of units with operational and modernization demands.
The ReARMM model establishes dedicated periods of time for mission, training, and modernization. All
units must understand the need to simultaneously balance or deconflict operational demands and mod-
ernize the force for large scale combat operations (LSCO). All aspects of training must modernize to sup-
port success in LSCO, reinforcing Soldiers as our first priority. This includes: our education systems
institutional schools and colleges; fitness; foundational skills and high-level competencies; combining live,
virtual, and synthetic environments; and improving Soldier and unit lethality.
Section III
Army Training and Leader Development Processes

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-8. The Army Training System
chunks.text:
Provide Army school-trained Soldiers, Army Civilians, and leaders who possess sound fundamen-
tals of their military or civilian specialty. Leaders have the responsibility to develop Soldiers and Army Ci-
vilians to achieve success on assigned missions. Leaders are also responsible for developing subordi-
nates to influence beyond the chain-of-command and accomplish potential future assignments of greater
responsibility.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-8. The Army Training System
chunks.text: Publish how-to-fight doctrine that provides the basis for effective, unified action.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-8. The Army Training System
chunks.text:
Provide training support products, services and facilities that enable leaders to plan, prepare, exe-
cute, and assess training to standard.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-8. The Army Training System
chunks.text:
Provide training activities through Combat Training Center (CTC) rotations that expose units to sub-
ject matter expertise.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-8. The Army Training System
chunks.text:
Provide resources, including synchronizing of training assets and activities, needed to execute train-
ing activities.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-8. The Army Training System
chunks.text:
Provide a TSS and Army training management system needed by the unit to plan, execute, and eval-
uate training and mission rehearsals and to assess operations and capture lessons learned.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-8. The Army Training System
chunks.text:
Leverage organizational approaches for integrating RA/RC units to meet current and contingency
demands.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-9. Authorities and responsibilities for training
chunks.text:
Training is an administrative control authority of the Army. In accordance with Title 10, United
States Code, Section 7013(b)(5) (10 USC 7013(b)(5)), the Secretary of the Army (SECARMY) is respon-
sible for training all Army forces, including those assigned to combatant commands (CCMD). Unit com-
manders are responsible for the training proficiency of their unit, and when required, for certifying training
readiness by unit and echelon. Training requirements include individual/crew-served/platform weapon
qualification, live-fire task proficiency, mission essential task proficiency, and an External Evaluation
(EXEVAL). Next higher headquarter commanders are responsible for confirming that units conduct train-
ing to standard and within prescribed time periods. The Army commands (ACOMs), Army service compo-
nent commands (ASCCs), and direct reporting units (DRUs) that provide forces for operational employ-
ment to a combatant commander (CCDR) will confirm units meet established Army deployment and em-
ployment criteria; and the respective ASCC (for ASCC/CCDR assigned conventional forces) and U.S.
Army Forces Command (FORSCOM) (for unassigned RA and RC conventional forces) will validate that
those forces meet training prerequisites before deployment. Unless otherwise directed by the Secretary of
Defense, CCDRs may employ RC Army forces assigned to their commands in contingency operations
only when validated by the Army force provider. Validation is not required for training, to include Overseas
Deployment Training (ODT) and State Partnership Program (SPP), unless designated as high risk or sen-
sitive by the requesting command (see also para 5-7).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-9. Authorities and responsibilities for training
chunks.text:
Training and readiness oversight. In contrast to the training validation responsibility of Army com-
manders, training and readiness oversight describes the limited authority of CCDRs over assigned RC
forces when not on active duty or when on Active-Duty Training (ADT) status. CCDRs normally exercise
training and readiness oversight through their Service Component commanders and FORSCOM (see
also para 2-20). The training and readiness oversight provides the authority to provide guidance on oper-
ational requirements and priorities addressed in military department training and readiness programs, ap-
prove participation by assigned RC forces in Joint training, review readiness inspections of assigned RC
forces, review mobilization plans (including post-mobilization training activities and deployability validation
procedures) developed for assigned RC forces, and comment on service program recommendations and
budget requests.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-10. The Army training management governance
chunks.text:
The Deputy Chief of Staff (DCS), G-3/5/7 manages Army training primarily through the Training General
Officer Steering Committee (TGOSC).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-10. The Army training management governance
chunks.text:
Structure. The TGOSC provides governance for the training enterprise and recommends improve-
ments in training policy and strategy, resourcing and capabilities needed to provide trained and ready Sol-
diers, leaders, Army Civilians, and units to the unified Forces of CCDRs. The TGOSC identifies and re-
solves issues, determines priorities, and makes decisions and recommendations in support of Army
Training and Leader Development to develop synchronized and integrated strategic recommendations for
the Chief of Staff of the Army (CSA) in support of Training and Army Readiness.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-10. The Army training management governance
chunks.text:
The DCS, G-3/5/7 chairs the TGOSC, with the DCS, G37/TR Director of Training (DOT) or Deputy
Director of Training (DDOT) serving as chair if the G-3/5/7 is absent. The DCS, G-3/5/7 (Director of
Training) and the DCS, G-1, (Director of Military Personnel Management (DMPM)), represent the Army
Staff (ARSTAF) as voting members; other ARSTAF may be invited.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-10. The Army training management governance
chunks.text:
Primary TGOSC members are also voting members and include general officers or Senior Execu-
tive Service (SES) members from each ACOM, ASCC, DRU, Army National Guard (ARNG), and USAR
who are responsible for training, education, and leader development. The TGOSC chair may invite other
Headquarters, Department of the Army (HQDA) and Joint Staff general officers or SES equivalent to at-
tend based on the issues being presented.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-10. The Army training management governance
chunks.text:
Generally, each 1&2-star TGOSC is followed by a 3-star TGOSC video teleconference (VTC) to
address key emerging or contentious topics that cannot be resolved at the 1-2 star TGOSC. The 3-star
TGOSC provides a back brief to the Army senior leaders concerning the outcome from the TGOSC cycle.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-10. The Army training management governance
chunks.text:
Meeting frequency. The TGOSC meets quarterly (or as required) to identify and resolve issues, de-
termine priorities, and make decisions and recommendations in support of Army Training and Leader De-
velopment providing synchronized and integrated strategic recommendations to Army senior leaders. A
number of Councils of Colonels (CoC), and subordinate workgroups, committees and boards support the
TGOSC to develop and frame training-related issues and emerging requirements for Army leaders’ deci-
sion.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-10. The Army training management governance
Chapter 1 > 1-10. The Army training management governance
chunks.text:
Training General Officer Steering Committee Integration Forum. The Integration Forum’s primary
function is to prepare the TGOSC agenda by integrating supporting CoCs and working group recommen-
dations forwarded by the supporting CoCs and working groups and to ensure the presentations of recom-
mendations are in the context of an Armywide training and leader development requirement. The DCS,
G-3/5/7 Deputy Director of Training, or designated colonel/GS-15 level representative chairs the Integra-
tion Forum. Integration Forum participants consist of the DCS, G-3/5/7 (Training Directorate) Colo-
nel/GS-15 chairs from each of the supporting COCs and TGOSC working groups. The DCS, G-3/5/7
Deputy Director of Training may invite ACOM, ASCC, DRU, ARNG, USAR, selected Army Staff, and
other representatives (subject matter experts) when needed.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-10. The Army training management governance
chunks.text:
Training General Officer Steering Committee supporting council of colonels and working
groups. The TGOSC chair will establish and provide guidance to supporting COC, such as: Operations
Portfolio COC, Mission COC, Institutional COC, and TSS COC; other COC may be established as re-
quired; such as the Army Munitions requirement COC. The chairperson for each COC will determine
work groups as required based on TGOSC guidance and recommendations from COC voting members.
Each COC chairperson will publish a charter, approved by the Director of Training, under which they will
operate.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-10. The Army training management governance
chunks.text:
Senior Leader Readiness Forum. The SLRF is an annual senior leader forum that provides the CSA
the opportunity to facilitate an informative dialogue among senior commanders and Army trainers on
changes necessary to support Army training and leader development policies and resourcing matters.
The SLRF focuses on current and future strategic training and leader development issues for the current
and future warrior leader.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-10. The Army training management governance
chunks.text:
The Training and Readiness Business Council. The T&R BC, chaired by the ADCS G-3/5/7, pro-
vides governance for the oversight and management of all T&R Domain IT investments and the integra-
tion and synchronization of activities related to T&R Domain Portfolio Management, Defense Business
System Certification, IT System Requirements, Data Architecture, Enterprise Architecture, End-to-End
Process Management (D2RR), and Global Force Management Data Initiative Implementation within the
Army.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-11. Training standardization
chunks.text: The objectives of training standardization are as follows:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-11. Training standardization
chunks.text: Ensure that organizations can operate effectively within any assigned formation.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-11. Training standardization
chunks.text: Ensure Soldiers and leaders can perform effectively within any assigned team.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-11. Training standardization
chunks.text:
Ensure common understanding of expected performance when planning, directing, requesting re-
sources, executing, and evaluating/assessing performance.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-11. Training standardization
chunks.text:
Performance is standardized by training to approved Army tasks, conditions, and standards. Collec-
tive and individual tasks and drills will comply with training and education standards and requirements in
accordance with TRADOC regulations governing individuals and unit level training. All training products
will be inputted into the Training Development Capability (TDC) validated in accordance with TRADOC
Regulation 350-70 and supporting TRADOC pamphlets, and accessible by units thru Army training Net-
work (ATN).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-11. Training standardization
chunks.text:
Standard Army ratings for task proficiency are: (T) is trained (advanced task proficiency), (P) is prac-
ticed (basic task proficiency), and (U) is untrained (cannot perform the task). These proficiency ratings are
applied to the assessment of all collective tasks.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-11. Training standardization
chunks.text:
The Army standard for evaluating collective task proficiency is to use the Training & Evaluation Out-
line (T&EO) for the collective task. Each task on a unit mission essential task list (METL) will include a
T&EO and be accessible to units through Army Training Network (ATN), Central Army Registry (CAR)
and Digital Training Management System (DTMS). Battalions, companies, and platoons must undergo an
EXEVAL annually (see para 5-3e). Echelons brigade and above should undergo an EXEVAL biannually
in conjunction with scheduled CTC rotations or at home station where possible to validate commander’s
assessment of training proficiency. An EXEVAL as defined in FM 7-0, is required for any unit to be as-
sessed as T on a task.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-11. Training standardization
chunks.text:
The Army standard for assessing overall unit training readiness is to use the unit METL and MET
proficiency, which results in an overall T-level (T1-T4) defined in AR 220-1. MET proficiency is assessed
by the unit commander and approved by the next higher commander during Commander-to-Commander
dialogue.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-11. Training standardization
chunks.text:
Weapons qualifications. Weapons qualification involves individual, crew-served, and platform
weapons proficiency. The unit achieves weapons qualification by qualifying on all its organic weapons
under all conditions as specified by applicable weapon system publications. Weapons qualifications are
valid for twelve months.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-11. Training standardization
chunks.text:
Collective live-fire task proficiency. Collective live-fire task proficiency is achieved by the unit exe-
cuting specified collective tasks while employing its organic weapons systems in a live-fire environment.
The collective task and conditions are established by commander-to-commander dialogue.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-11. Training standardization
chunks.text:
Training days. The T-days estimates the number of continuous training days, unconstrained by re-
sources, required to achieve T1 (fully trained). T-days is derived from the unit’s approved training plan
and refined through commander-to-commander dialogue.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-11. Training standardization
chunks.text:
Report standardization issues to the appropriate U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
(TRADOC) proponents (see AR 5-22), including:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-11. Training standardization
chunks.text: Procedures that are performed differently by similar units despite standard guidance.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-11. Training standardization
chunks.text: Procedures that units perform differently than the training base.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-11. Training standardization
chunks.text: Operations and maintenance procedures for equipment that vary despite similarities in equipment.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-11. Training standardization
chunks.text: Directed procedures that vary from established guidance and should be standardized.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-11. Training standardization
chunks.text: Missing, incorrect, or inadequate procedures.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-11. Training standardization
chunks.text: Unresolved standardization issues will be reported to Commanding General (CG), TRADOC, who-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-11. Training standardization
chunks.text:
Establishes integration and standardization programs to ensure compatibility of training and doc-
trine.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-11. Training standardization
chunks.text: Identifies integration items for inclusion in standardization programs.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-11. Training standardization
chunks.text:
Conducts integration and standardization team assistance visits to divisions or higher units in coor-
dination with ASCC/RA commanders.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-11. Training standardization
chunks.text: Advises DCS, G-3/5/7 on matters pertaining to standardization.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-11. Training standardization
chunks.text:
Tasks the appropriate TRADOC staff or proponent school to develop training solutions for stand-
ardization issues.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-11. Training standardization
chunks.text:
Common mandatory training (CMT) will comply with training and education standards and require-
ments in accordance with this AR and TRADOC regulations governing institutional and unit level training.
All CMT training products will be input into the Training Development Capability (TDC), validated in ac-
cordance with TRADOC 350-70 and supporting TRADOC pamphlets, and accessible by units thru ATN
(see app C).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-11. Training standardization
chunks.text:
2-star commanders (or equivalent senior executive service officials), and state adjutants general
(TAG) are delegated the authority by the Secretary of the Army and the DCS, G-3/5/7 to exempt units
from mandatory training requirements (unless prohibited by law or Secretary of the Army decision) in in-
stances where the unit can demonstrate insufficient time available to achieve readiness requirements.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-12. Training domains
chunks.text:
The Army’s training and leader development efforts support training and education in the three training
domains: institutional, operational, and self-development. All the domains have an important role in train-
ing Soldiers and Army Civilians, growing leaders, and preparing units for employment. ADP 7-0 provides
further discussion on the training domains.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-12. Training domains
chunks.text:
The institutional training domain. The institutional training domain includes Army centers/schools
that provide initial training and subsequent functional and professional military education and training for
Soldiers, military leaders, and Army Civilians. Army schools help instill the Army Profession, the Army
Ethic, and character development of Army professionals. The institutional training domain also provides
training support products, information, and materials needed by individuals for self-development and by
unit leaders in the operational training domain to accomplish training and mission rehearsal/assessment.
The institution is a key enabler for unit readiness, providing initial military training, reclassification/military
occupational specialty (MOS)-transition, subsequent professional military education and Civilian educa-
tion and direct support to units for functional training through access to training task development data
bases, mobile training teams (MTTs) or other means. The institutional training domain takes lessons
learned from operational deployments and the operational training domain and updates doctrine and tac-
tics, techniques, and procedures and then disseminates this information back to the field; or modifies in-
stitutional instruction to address gaps in learning.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-12. Training domains
chunks.text:
The operational training domain. The operational training domain encompasses training activities
that unit leaders schedule, and individuals, units and organizations undertake. Commanders are respon-
sible for unit training readiness from individual to unit collective level proficiency. This responsibility ap-
plies individual Soldiers, Army Civilians, and leaders at all levels. Experience in the operational domain is
essential for leader development. Leaders must allow their subordinates adequate time to pursue educa-
tion and self-development opportunities. Subordinate leaders assist commanders in achieving training
readiness proficiency goals by ensuring training is conducted to standard in support of the Army Standard
METL. These activities include progressive training conducted at home station, regional collective training
capability, regional training centers, and mobilization centers; Joint Pacific Multinational Readiness Cen-
ter (JPMRC) U.S. Army Pacific (USARPAC), Combat Support Training Program (CSTP) exercises
(CSTX/WAREX) and Combat Training Center (CTC) rotations; during Joint exercises; and while region-
ally aligned or operationally deployed. For the Reserve Component forces, the operational domain in-
cludes reserve centers, armories, and state training areas and facilities. For units in the operational Army,
METL-based strategies (known as CATS) are synchronized with the weapon training strategy to build and
sustain unit readiness.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-12. Training domains
chunks.text:
Self-development training domain. The self-development domain includes planned and goal-ori-
ented learning that reinforces and expands the depth and breadth of an individual’s knowledge base and
self-awareness. Self-development bridges learning gaps between the operational and institutional do-
mains and sets conditions for continuous learning and growth. Leaders must limit their desire to direct
subordinates to pursue specific fields of study for self-development, and then encourage and expect that
subordinates seek knowledge on a topic or field of study that interests them. Self-development will com-
plement what has been learned in the classroom and on the job, enhance professional competence, and
help meet personal objectives.
Section IV
Army Training Programs

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-13. Oversight of Army training programs
chunks.text:
The ASA (M&RA) is responsible for setting the strategic direction for and ensuring Army policies, plans,
and programs for training and reserve affairs are executed consistent with law, regulation, and policy. The
DCS, G-3/5/7 approves all Army training programs and provides oversight for several program areas.
The DCS, G-3/5/7 is assisted by the Director of Training, designated HQDA lead agents, and the
TGOSC. These programs apply to both the Operating Force and Generating Force. Consistent with the
training management principles in ADP 7-0/FM 7-0 and ADP 6-22/FM 6-22, HQDA as a general policy
strives to decentralize responsibilities for determining unit training requirements to the unit commander,
who is best postured to analyze the unit’s training status and plan training events maximizing TSS capa-
bilities available to the unit. HQDA establishes training requirements and program guidance when im-
portant to ensure a particular force capability, equity, or standard.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-13. Oversight of Army training programs
chunks.text:
The Combined Arms Training Strategy (CATS) Program provides a metric useful for HQDA to deter-
mine operational tempo (OPTEMPO) and training resourcing requirements for the program objective
memorandum (POM), as well as unit strategies to assist leaders in planning training events to incorporate
into the unit training plan.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-13. Oversight of Army training programs
chunks.text:
DTMS is the required automated system to use to assist the commander/trainer in planning and
tracking individual and collective unit training tasks; and is also the authoritative data system for specific
data elements. In the future, DTMS will be replaced by the Army Training Information System (ATIS). All
subsequent references to training program requirements within this publication for ATMS (DTMS and
ATN) will transition to ATIS as it is fielded.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-13. Oversight of Army training programs
chunks.text:
The Army Training and Evaluation Program (ARTEP) is DCS, G-3/5/7’s program for collective train-
ing. ARTEP assists HQDA and ACOMs by providing the operational force a management process to re-
view and provide input to operational domain collective products, training development policy and Army
training policy. This program ensures collective training product compliance with Army senior leader guid-
ance, current doctrine, organizational changes, and unit training readiness reporting requirements. Gov-
erned by the STRAG, ARTEP ensures commander and leaders have relevant mission focused training
products and tools to enable them to effectively prioritize training and optimize limited training time and
resources.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-13. Oversight of Army training programs
chunks.text:
The Standards for Training Readiness Advisory Group (STRAG) provides a management process to
review Proponent recommended Standard Mission Essential Task Lists (METL) for the DCS, G-3/5/7 and
govern ARTEP. The STRAG also provides a forum to discuss other aspects of training, to include ARTEP
as deemed appropriate by HQDA.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-14. Specific program areas
chunks.text: Institutional Training (see chap 3).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-14. Specific program areas
chunks.text: Civilian Training (see chap 4).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-14. Specific program areas
chunks.text: Training in units (see chap 5).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-14. Specific program areas
chunks.text: Training Support Systems (see chap 6) and AR 350-52.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-14. Specific program areas
chunks.text: Modernization Training (see chap 7) and AR 525-29.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-14. Specific program areas
chunks.text: Physical Readiness/Army Fitness Test (AFT) (see chap 3 and app C).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2
chunks.text: Responsibilities

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-1. Assistant Secretary of the Army (Acquisition, Logistics and Technology)
chunks.text: Through assigned program executive office (PEO) and/or program manager (PMs)-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-1. Assistant Secretary of the Army (Acquisition, Logistics and Technology)
chunks.text:
Provide new equipment training (NET) funding for each assigned system in accordance with the
approved capability requirements document, approved training activities, approved program baseline, and
decisions made during the milestone reviews governed by AR 70-1.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-1. Assistant Secretary of the Army (Acquisition, Logistics and Technology)
chunks.text: Coordinate NET strategies, management, and the conduct of NET with-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-1. Assistant Secretary of the Army (Acquisition, Logistics and Technology)
chunks.text:
The U.S. Army Materiel Command (AMC) NET managers and AMC training developers (TNG-
DEVs).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-1. Assistant Secretary of the Army (Acquisition, Logistics and Technology)
chunks.text:
The proponent (DA-designated or TRADOC-designated) TNGDEVs for approval of all NET prod-
ucts.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-1. Assistant Secretary of the Army (Acquisition, Logistics and Technology)
chunks.text:
The TRADOC Combined Arms Center Training to ensure NET products support individual and col-
lective tasks.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-1. Assistant Secretary of the Army (Acquisition, Logistics and Technology)
chunks.text:
Coordinate Army modernization training (AMT) policy issues with DCS, G-3/5/7 (Training Direc-
torate), Washington, DC 20310-0450. Field new equipment and equipment upgrades in accordance with
the Army Resourcing Priority List and DCS, G-3/5/7 and DCS, G-8 priorities and schedules in order to
support the Generating Force and Operating Force.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-1. Assistant Secretary of the Army (Acquisition, Logistics and Technology)
chunks.text:
Formulate and manage policies governing the training and education programs for Army Acquisition
Workforce (AAW) personnel and generating force/operating force regarding operational contract support
skills and knowledge. Serve as HQDA point of contact for career development of AAW personnel.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-1. Assistant Secretary of the Army (Acquisition, Logistics and Technology)
chunks.text:
Coordinate with the ASA (M&RA); DCS, G-3/5/7; DCS, G-1; The Army Office of the General Coun-
sel; The Judge Advocate General (TJAG); Chief, National Guard Bureau; Director, Army National Guard
(DARNG); Chief, Army Reserve (CAR); and Commander, Human Resources Command (HRC), on policy
issues governing AAW military and Civilian training and education.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-1. Assistant Secretary of the Army (Acquisition, Logistics and Technology)
chunks.text:
Coordinate with HRC and DARNG for administration of quotas for Officer Education System (OES)
and/or Noncommissioned Officer Professional Development System (NCOPDS), certification, and func-
tional area training courses for RA personnel.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-1. Assistant Secretary of the Army (Acquisition, Logistics and Technology)
chunks.text: Through the Director, Acquisition Career Management (DACM).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-1. Assistant Secretary of the Army (Acquisition, Logistics and Technology)
chunks.text:
Assist the AAE in implementing Defense Acquisition Workforce Improvement Act (DAWIA) (Title
10, United States Code, Section 1701 (10 USC 1701)) provisions. This includes high-quality education,
training, and other career broadening programs to enhance technical competencies and leadership skills
of persons serving in acquisition positions.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-1. Assistant Secretary of the Army (Acquisition, Logistics and Technology)
chunks.text:
Manage and implement the Army Career Development Program for AAW personnel in coordination
with the Office of the Secretary of Defense (OSD), Director, Acquisition Career Management (DACM).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-1. Assistant Secretary of the Army (Acquisition, Logistics and Technology)
chunks.text:
Establish and publish education, training, and experience standards for each acquisition position
category and acquisition functional area. Standards will be designated either mandatory or desired, in
conjunction with OSD functional boards and functional advisors.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-1. Assistant Secretary of the Army (Acquisition, Logistics and Technology)
chunks.text:
Identify and publish career paths for Army military and Army Civilian acquisition personnel. Career
paths will include the mandatory and desired education, training, experience, and recommended assign-
ments necessary for progression to the most senior acquisition positions.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-1. Assistant Secretary of the Army (Acquisition, Logistics and Technology)
chunks.text:
Assist with implementing improvements regarding development of non-acquisition operational con-
tract support skills and knowledge. This includes education, training and other mission enhancing pro-
grams.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-1. Assistant Secretary of the Army (Acquisition, Logistics and Technology)
chunks.text:
Oversee the development, coordination and implementation of policy and programs for the Army Se-
curity Assistance Program. Develop Army input to security assistance programs in coordination with the
Army staff.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-1. Assistant Secretary of the Army (Acquisition, Logistics and Technology)
chunks.text:
Exercise HQDA responsibility for developing policy concerning foreign training programs under in-
ternational military education and training; foreign military sales; foreign military financing; international
narcotics and law enforcement affairs; and nonproliferation, antiterrorism (AT), de-mining, and related
matters.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-2. Assistant Secretary of the Army (Installations, Energy and Environment)
chunks.text:
Oversee policy for safety and occupational health (SOH) training of all commanders, leaders, man-
agers, supervisors, Soldiers, civilians, and contractors.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-2. Assistant Secretary of the Army (Installations, Energy and Environment)
chunks.text: Provide oversite of the effectiveness of SOH training at all levels across the Army.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-2. Assistant Secretary of the Army (Installations, Energy and Environment)
chunks.text: Serve as the proponent for all SOH training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-2. Assistant Secretary of the Army (Installations, Energy and Environment)
chunks.text:
Develop, validate, resource, and manage SOH information technology to track all SOH hazard spe-
cific training across the Army.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Assistant Secretary of the Army (Manpower and Reserve Affairs)
chunks.text: Serve as the TT-PEG co-chair with the DCS, G-3/5/7.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Assistant Secretary of the Army (Manpower and Reserve Affairs)
chunks.text: Recommend training priorities for programming decisions and budget execution to the SECARMY.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Assistant Secretary of the Army (Manpower and Reserve Affairs)
chunks.text:
Exercise oversight for the development of requirements and resourcing requests to ensure con-
sistency with SECARMY’s guidance.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Assistant Secretary of the Army (Manpower and Reserve Affairs)
chunks.text:
Oversee policies for electronic-based distributed learning (EBDL) to ensure compliance with statu-
tory responsibilities and Department of Defense (DoD) requirements.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Assistant Secretary of the Army (Manpower and Reserve Affairs)
chunks.text: Is the approval authority of the EBDL course list.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Assistant Secretary of the Army (Manpower and Reserve Affairs)
chunks.text: Is the approval authority for course waivers submitted by the DCS G-3/5/7.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Assistant Secretary of the Army (Manpower and Reserve Affairs)
chunks.text: Serve as policy proponent for financial literacy program.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Assistant Secretary of the Army (Manpower and Reserve Affairs)
chunks.text:
Identify training courses to ensure integration of Army Civilians into existing military schools. Maxim-
ize the use of TRADOC and other military schools as planned resources for Civilian functional training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Assistant Secretary of the Army (Manpower and Reserve Affairs)
chunks.text:
Develop, validate, resource, and manage an Army Civilian Training Student Account (CTSA) to sup-
port resident Senior Service College (SSC) participants.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-4. The Inspector General
chunks.text:
Conduct, as appropriate, Armywide inspections related to training policy, and the impacts of training
on the readiness and warfighting capabilities of all Army units.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-4. The Inspector General
chunks.text: Conduct inspector general training and sustainment courses in accordance with AR 20-1.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-5. Chief Information Officer
chunks.text:
Exercise HQDA responsibility concerning training for systems that provide network connectivity in
both the operational and garrison environments.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-5. Chief Information Officer
chunks.text:
Exercise HQDA responsibility for information assurance training and develop policy related to the
training and certification of information assurance personnel (for example, system administrator, network
administrator, and information assurance security officer).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-5. Chief Information Officer
chunks.text:
Develop a certification process that validates integrated unit information technology (IT) configura-
tion. This certification applies to the initial fielding and subsequent upgrades of hardware, software, and
training devices.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Supervise HRC in managing the professional development of active-duty Soldiers, to include the
following:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text: Select and schedule active-duty Soldiers for attendance at Army schools.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Implement policy governing the use of training results within the Enlisted Personnel Management
System (EPMS).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Assists the ASA (M&RA) in the development of policy for the direction, control, and approval of the
curriculum for the U.S. Military Academy (USMA) and the USMA Preparatory School.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Implement plans, implement policies, and programs for the administration of the junior and senior
Reserve Officers’ Training Corps (ROTC) programs, and the National Defense Cadet Corps (NDCC).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Coordinate with the Superintendent, USMA to assure standardized training in all commissioning
sources.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Determine the specialty, MOS, career management field classification, and enlistment and reenlist-
ment criteria of grade structure requirements for the Army; provide this data to DCS, G-3/5/7 (Training
Directorate), to assist in establishing training base requirements.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Develop and administer the Army’s military personnel management system in coordination with ap-
propriate agencies.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Review NET and DET plans for personnel requirements and shortfalls. Provide comments and rec-
ommended solutions to materiel developer (MATDEV) and proponent (TRADOC or DA if applicable)
TNGDEV, as appropriate.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text: Ensure timely and accurate MOS decisions.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Provide personnel assessments and distribution priorities to the USAR and ACOM pertaining to ma-
teriel fielding plans.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Review near-term personnel plans, policies, and programs to ensure they support ACOM personnel
requirements with the correct MOS for new systems.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Develop personnel policy guidance and procedures for the development of institutional training re-
quirements for the Army in accordance with AR 350-10.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text: Manage ATRRS in accordance with AR 350-10, in coordination with DCS, G-3/5/7.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text: Provide guidance and direction for the operation of the Civilian Human Resources Agency.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text: Manage the Army’s Enterprise Civilian Talent Acquisition Programs (see also AR 690-950).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Support The Army Distributed Learning Program (TADLP) in accordance with guidance from DCS,
G-3/5/7:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text: Develop Army personnel policy guidance for distributed learning (DL) instructional activities.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Provide direct access for training enrollment to Soldiers and Army Civilians through the ATRRS DL
website for all self-development training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Support DCS, G-2 and DCS, G-3/5/7, in reviewing the strategic language list for training, consistent
with AR 11-6.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text: Under DCS, G-1 staff supervision, the CG, HRC will-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Serve as the principal agent for the DCS, G-1 staff supervision, in determining regular duty peace-
time and mobilization training-seat requirements.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Manage the selected flow of Regular Army personnel into Army schools and training centers. Se-
lect personnel to attend Army schools, non-Army schools, and training centers. Manage selected RA per-
sonnel attending Army schools, non-Army schools and training centers in ATRRS in accordance with
TASS. Manage selected RA personnel to attend Army RC training institutions, as appropriate, to leverage
all means of TASS.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Schedule and assign active-duty officers to captain career courses (CCCs) (except officers within
the six Corps of the U.S. Army Medical Departments, Chaplain Corps, and Judge Advocate General’s
Corps (JAGC)).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Manage quotas and select active duty AMEDD officers, warrant officers, and enlisted personnel to
attend designated schools and courses. Assign active-duty officers to BOLC, AMEDD Officer Basic
Leader Course, and/or the AMEDD Officer Career Course.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Manage the Advanced Civil Schooling Program. Coordinate with DCS, G-3/5/7 (Training Direc-
torate), to develop RA training requirements for specialized skill and professional military education
courses.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Review NET plans and DET plans for personnel requirements and shortfalls and, in coordination
with DCS, G-1, provide comments and recommended solutions to MATDEVs and TNGDEVs, when ap-
propriate.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text: Manage the Officer Candidate School (OCS) Program.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Provide administrative support for the ROTC and NDCC programs; manage branch and basic entry
specialty selection and selection programs for ROTC graduates.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Develop and manage training seat requirements for functional training courses for active-duty per-
sonnel.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Manage WO candidate accessions and schedule attendance at Warrant Officer Candidate School
(WOCS) and Warrant Officer Basic Course (WOBC). Manage quotas and schedule active-duty WO at-
tendance at Warrant Officer Advanced Course (WOAC), Warrant Officer Intermediate Level Education
(WOILE), and Warrant Officer Senior Service Education (WOSSE). Select and assign WOs to serve as
WOCS training, advising, and counseling officers.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Develop and manage, in compliance with DCS, G-3/5/7, the Advanced Leaders Course (ALC)
and Senior Leaders Course (SLC) training seat requirements.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text: Schedule RA Soldiers to attend ALC and SLC.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Support the Sergeants Major Course (SMC), selection system (including the resident, non-resi-
dent courses) and manage active-duty training seats for the SMC.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Develop policy governing the Academic Evaluation Reporting System for officer and enlisted stu-
dents.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text: Exercise responsibility for AR 11-6 and-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Develop personnel management systems and procedures for the Army Foreign Language Program
(see AR 11-16).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Coordinate with the DCS, G-2, and users of AR 11-6 to manage the career program for WOs and
enlisted linguist personnel.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Develop, coordinate, and issue technical and regulatory guidance concerning management of the
Army Learning Center and APT programs.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text: Schedule USAR officers for CCC, ILE and SSC Course.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text: Schedule USAR warrant officers for WOCS, WOBC, WOAC, WOILE, and WOSSE.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text: Manage selected USAR personnel attending non-Army schools in ATRRS.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text: Develop and manage RA officers to attend the CCC, ILE and SSC.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Develop the BOLC training seat requirements to meet Army officer accession requirements in
compliance with DCS, G-1.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text: Support TADLP-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text: Participate in DL course reviews to validate course impact on personnel readiness.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Identify through the DCS, G-1 to the training ACOMs, additional or alternative DL courses to meet
personnel readiness requirements.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Identify critical personnel readiness requirements that can be supported by DL and recommend pri-
oritization of total Army manning requirements via SMDR and TRAP processes.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Implements Army personnel policy and entitlement guidance for electronic-based DL compensation
based on priorities set by the DCS, G-3/5/7.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Identifies personnel readiness requirements supported by electronic-based DL and coordinates the
prioritization of efforts with the DCS, G-3/5/7.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text: Provides Soldiers direct access for enrollment through the ATRRS electronic-based DL website.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Is responsible for appropriately annotating all approved electronic-based DL courses within the
ATRRS.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Ensure HRC will participate in the Training Coordination Council Workgroup (TCCW) and provide
full support for training program execution.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text: Participate in the TGOSC process.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text: The Director, SLDO will-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Manage the Regular Army population of general officers, O-6s and promotable O-5s in accord-
ance with established laws and policies, who have attended developmental opportunities at the direction
of Army senior leadership to meet national security requirements, (except officers within the Medical
Corps, Dental Corps, Veterinarian Corps, Chaplain Corps, Acquisition Corps and JAGC).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Manage colonels and promotable lieutenant colonels and program officers in coordination with
HRC and HQDA, DCS, G-3/5/7 (Training Directorate) for CSA Senior Army Fellowships, Senior Service
College (SSC), USAWC Fellowships, nonmilitary education level (MEL)-1 fellowships, internships, Army
schools, and non-Army schools.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Manage officers selected to command at the general officer and colonel level ensuring completion
of appropriate command preparation development opportunities.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Institute future programs at the strategic level to encourage officer development; thereby, advanc-
ing the inherent talents, experiences, and leadership skills of a combined force of senior leaders at the
enterprise level.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text: CHRA will-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Assist the ASA (M&RA) with formulating policies, program resources, and overseeing the imple-
mentation of policy and programs for Army Civilian leader development programs to include the Civilian
Education System and the Army Civilian Training, Education and Development System (ACTEDS), and
career long assessments.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Identify effective and efficient delivery of training to meet training requirements for the Army Civilian
Corps.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Implements and manage training policy and training procedures for Army Civilians in military and
civilian institutions.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Manage the enterprise-level Army Civilian training requirements to meet the short and long-term
Army and DoD training management goals/objectives.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Validate Civilian functional training requirements identified by the DA Office of the Assistant G-1 for
Civilian Personnel (AG-1(CP)), functional chief/functional chief advisors ACOMs, ASCCs, and DRUs. De-
velop programming and budget data.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Allocate HQDA ECTAP training funds for competitive professional development (CPD) based on
validated training requirements. Manage the execution of those funds.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text: Validate eligibility of CP and command nominations for competitive training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Prioritize training allocations for constrained courses during the year of execution and during the
POM years.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text: Serve as a member of the Civilian Personnel Policy Committee.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Report competitive training to OSD, Office of Personnel Management (OPM) and other key stake-
holders, as required.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Manage Army Civilian competitive professional development operations and enforcement manage-
ment control procedures. Distribute HQDA central training funds for CPD based on validated training re-
quirements. Manage and monitor the execution of funds through financial databases and provide periodic
updates, as required.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text: Serve as the Army Staff proponent for managing all mandatory Army Civilian training requirements.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-1
chunks.text:
Serve as the Army’s liaison to OPM and DoD, as delegated by the ASA (M&RA), to ensure compli-
ance with and implementation of training and leader development policy and program development as
defined by OMB directives and statutory requirements. Monitor impacting legislation and coordinate with
AG-1(CP). Maintain civilian training data, an enterprise training management system, and report to OSD,
OPM and congress as directed. Ensure the integration of Army Civilians into existing military leader de-
velopment programs wherever practical.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-7. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-2
chunks.text:
Exercise HQDA responsibility for the functional aspects of officer, WO, enlisted, and Army Civilian
intelligence and counterintelligence specialist training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-7. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-2
chunks.text:
Exercise HQDA oversight for developing policy related to institutional and force intelligence and se-
curity training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-7. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-2
chunks.text:
Exercise HQDA oversight for developing policy related to opposing forces (OPFOR) training in ac-
cordance with AR 350-2.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-7. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-2
chunks.text: Exercise HQDA responsibility for the Army Foundry Intelligence Training Program (see AR 350-32).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-7. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-2
chunks.text:
Serve as the HQDA representative on the Army on the Department of Defense Intelligence Training
and Education Board (DITEB) in accordance with DoDI 3115.11.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-7. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-2
chunks.text:
Exercise HQDA responsibility for developing policy related to foreign disclosure in accordance with
AR 380-10, within the institutional training domain.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text:
Approve Army military and Army Civilian training and education programs and serve as the Army
Staff proponent for all HQDA mandatory training requirements. Approve and publish a list of general sub-
ject areas in which Soldiers and leaders must possess a required level of knowledge and skills. Approve
and add to the HQDA mandatory training list all training requirements directed by law, DoD, the
SECARMY, the Army Staff, and agencies that have the authority to publish directives that include training
requirements.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text:
Coordinate with CG, TRADOC; the CG, U.S. Army Cadet Command for ROTC; the Commandant,
U.S. Army Infantry School (USAIS) for Federal OCS; the DARNG for state-operated OCS; and the U.S.
Army Warrant Officer Career College (USAWOCC) to assure standardized training in all commissioning
and appointment sources.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text:
Approve ARTEP products HQDA standard METL for Army units. Ensure Standard METLs reflect the
unit’s designated, core capabilities. Ensure Army training resource requirements defined by METL-based
CATS are synchronized so that training is resourced, and the Army can train to standard.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text:
Serve as the chair of the Standards in training commission (STRAC) in accordance with DA Pam
350-38. Serve as chair of the Standards for Training Readiness Advisory Group (STRAG) in accordance
with the STRAG Charter.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text:
Serve as proponent for the Army SRP. Formulate policies for planning, programming, operating, and
managing ranges and training lands.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text: Serve as Army policy and resource proponent for leader development (training and education).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text:
Establish new training strategies that accommodate the emergence of new Armywide workforce
development programs, distributed learning, delegated authority and its effect on training program data
analysis, evaluation, and cost effectiveness.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text: Co-chair the SMDR process with DCS, G-1 to build an executable and affordable ARPRINT.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text:
Co-chair the TRAP process with DCS, G-1 to adjust the ARPRINT during the budget year and year
of execution.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text:
Program resources for range modernization and major training land acquisition projects determined
to be a new mission requirement in accordance with AR 140-483, AR 350-19, AR 420-1, and DA Pam
420-1-2.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text: Advise the Chief of Staff, Army (CSA), on unified action training programs.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text:
Validate Joint training requirements for officer professional military education, as required by CJCSI
1800.01F.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text:
Exercise HQDA responsibility for training Soldier and leader tasks and establishing policy for Army
Warrior Training (AWT).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text:
Exercise HQDA responsibility for Soldier training courses; the Army Physical Fitness Program and
Holistic Health and Fitness System (H2F); weapons qualification; chemical, biological, radiological, and
nuclear (CBRN) defense training; Personnel Recovery (PR) training; training in the code of conduct; sur-
vival, evasion, resistance, and escape (SERE); Army amphibious training; and Army modernization train-
ing.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text: Monitor Joint Airborne/Air Transportability Training (JA/ATT) Program (see app E).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text:
Exercise HQDA responsibility and publish guidelines for RC pre- mobilization and post-mobilization
training requirements.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text:
Establish policy for training and education programs for military personnel in Civilian and industrial
institutions.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text: Coordinate with the TRADOC on development of policies and programs for ACES.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text: Exercise HQDA responsibility for and develop policy for the DCS, G-3/5/7 Strategist Program.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text: Exercise HQDA responsibility and serve as the point of contact for the following:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text: Defense Language Steering Committee.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text: National Defense University.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text: The in-country training phase of the Foreign Area Officers Program.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text: Information Resource Management College.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text: Institute for National Strategic Studies.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text: Staff and SSCs of Other Nations program.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text: Army Fellowships (see AR 621-7).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text: Provide HQDA representation for the Defense Management Education and Training Board.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text: Provide distribution plans for displaced equipment to the Army staff and the ACOMs.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text:
Provide program and budget guidance for the operation of Army Learning Centers and the APT, to
include printing and publishing language aptitude and proficiency tests for use throughout DoD.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text:
Plan, execute, and manage foreign military training programs outside the Security Assistance Pro-
gram.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text: Chair the Army TGOSC, through the Director of Training to-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text: Schedule COCs, and Work Groups in support of TGOSC efforts.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text: Direct the training portions of the operations and maintenance, Army (OMA) program and budget.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text: Monitor the training portions of the RA, ARNG and USAR budgets.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text: Program funds to support approved Army training strategies and master plans.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text:
Approve ACOM proposals for contracted Soldier training courses in coordination with representa-
tives from other Army staff elements and the proponent ACOM.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text:
Validate and integrate institutional training, unit training, and leader development requirements into
unit set fielding (USF).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text: Serve as the HQDA proponent for AMT and related support facilities.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text: Coordinate AMT issues with HQDA staff agencies.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text:
Ensure NET requirements are planned and funded to support Army warfighting experiments, ad-
vanced technology demonstrations, and warfighting accelerated or urgent acquisition initiatives.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text: Serve as the HQDA proponent for TADLP.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text: Exercise HQDA responsibility for the Defense Foreign Language Program for military personnel.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text:
Supervise the resourcing, programming and assist with budgeting activities to support the institu-
tional training except for Defense health Agency (DHA) funded medical courses.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text:
Develop policy for the management and operation of training support centers, except for policy
governing visual information functions.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text:
Serve as the HQDA proponent and provide management for the Army’s Institutional Training Travel
Program (ITTDY) except for those funded/administered by DHA, DCS, G-1, and U.S. Army Special Oper-
ations Command (USASOC).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text:
Exercise HQDA responsibility for establishment of institutional training travel policies, procedures,
and standards for executing institutional training travel orders.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text: Program and provide management oversight of institutional training travel funds.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text:
Exercise HQDA responsibility for the development, coordination, and implementation of training
policies, priorities, and programs in support of the Army’s institutional training mission.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text:
Determine and direct quarters and subsistence support for Soldiers attending institutional training in
coordination with HQDA Staff agencies.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text:
Serve as proponent and provide management oversight for ITDLM Program. Program training sup-
port funds to U.S. Army Corps of Engineer (USACE) and AMC with instructions to use the funds to sup-
port RA resident student lodging and directed meal requirements under the ITDLM Program.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text: Provide management oversight and funding for the ATMS programs.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text:
Serve as the Army’s senior language authority. Assist DCS, G-2 in determining the required coun-
tries, cultures, and languages, to include reviewing the strategic language list for service concurrence.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text: Support TADLP-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text:
Provide HQDA oversight for DL. Responsibilities include policies and procedures required to sup-
port implementation and sustainment of DL.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text:
Exercise HQDA responsibility for Soldier training and education courses, including those conducted
with DL, and provide guidance to the Army on training and educating Army personnel within a DL environ-
ment.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text: Resource DL through the planning, programming, budgeting and execution (PPBE) process.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text:
Coordinate with DCS, G-1, support, and fund the development and implementation of all neces-
sary interfaces among DL, personnel management systems, and ATMS.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text:
Support Electronic-Based DL (EBDL) training. Establishes priorities for EBDL. Serves as the propo-
nent for the Army EBDL program, including policies and procedures required to support implementation
and sustainment of EBDL. Coordinates with the DCS, G-1 in developing policies, procedures, and pro-
grams for EBDL. Provides guidance on operating an EBDL training environment and ensures that training
development policies are standardized. Responsible for developing the list of authorized and eligible
EBDL courses. Responsible for submission of all course waivers to ASA (M&RA). Validate the EBDL
course list via the Institutional Portfolio CoC.
aa. Support Electronic-Based DL (EBDL) training.
bb. Co-chair the SMDR and participate as a voting member in the TRAP.
cc. Participate in the Training Coordination Council Workshop (TCCW). Coordinate the resolution of all
major training mission resource issues and constraints presented to the TCCW.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text: Ensure resources follow the mission across components.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text:
Co-chair the SMDR ensuring that the output from the TCCW is incorporated in the requirement ver-
ification process for the out years.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text:
Co-chair, along with TRADOC, a workgroup of Army stakeholders to review current institutional
training force structure requirements to determine the right structure to better support the institutional
training mission and total enduring institutional training TDA requirements and authorizations.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-3/5/7
chunks.text:
Ensure DCS, G-3/5/7, DCS G-37/TR in coordination with ARNG and USAR, will account for the
total instructor and training support personnel requirements for RC TASS institutions.
dd. Serve as the proponent for the Army Driver and Operator Standardization Program (see AR 600-
55).
ee. As required, designate Army training and education proponents in instances where CG, TRADOC
has not already appointed modernization and branch proponents responsible for the development of unit
and individual training and the development of TSPs and products for approved training requirements.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-9. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-4
chunks.text:
Ensure integrated product and sustainment support requirements are validated and included in ma-
teriel acquisition processes to support USF and full materiel release of programs and systems.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-9. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-4
chunks.text:
Monitor, assess, and recommend to the DCS, G-3/5/7 (Training Directorate) appropriate action on
AMT as part of integrated product support.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-9. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-4
chunks.text:
Review NET Plans and DET Plans for logistical impacts. Provide comments to MATDEVs and/or
TNGDEVs, as appropriate.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-9. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-4
chunks.text:
Exercise HQDA responsibility to ensure the support and maintenance of fielded training support
products, as required.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-9. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-4
chunks.text: Support equipment fill at appropriate requirement levels across the operational readiness cycles.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-10. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-6
chunks.text:
Exercise HQDA responsibility concerning training for systems that provide network connectivity in
both the operational and garrison environments.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-10. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-6
chunks.text:
Exercise HQDA responsibility for information assurance training and develop policy related to the
training and certification of information assurance personnel (for example, system administrator, network
administrator, and information assurance security officer).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-11. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-8
chunks.text:
Develop, independently assess, integrate, and synchronize The Army Program in support of The
Army Vision and Army Campaign Plan (ACP).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-11. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-8
chunks.text:
Serve as the principal advisor to the CSA on Joint materiel capabilities integration for all doctrine,
organizations, training, materiel, leadership and education, personnel, facilities, and policy (DOTMLPF-P)
requirements and materiel programs execution over their life cycles.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-11. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-8
chunks.text:
Develop and defend the Army POM and the Future Years Defense Program; includes the independ-
ent assessment, integration, and synchronization of the Army POM.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-11. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-8
chunks.text:
Publish POM-build guidance that is capability-centric and based on modernizing or transforming by
unit sets.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-11. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-8
chunks.text:
Integrate the Program evaluation group (PEG) submissions during the POM build to ensure USF
success.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-11. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-8
chunks.text: Serve as the USF system-of-systems manager in execution of Army transformation.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-11. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-8
chunks.text: Coordinate with ACOMs to validate unit set configurations.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-11. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-8
chunks.text: Approve unit set equipment lists.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-11. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-8
chunks.text:
Support the production, delivery and integration of training and training support for USF sustainable
readiness requirements to include delivery of first production or procurement items of new equipment to
proponent schoolhouses.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-11. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-8
chunks.text:
Develop a risk assessment and management process to gain early visibility into technical and pro-
gram issues affecting program integration, interoperability, and scheduling.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-11. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-8
chunks.text:
Develop and maintain software-blocking policy and oversee the implementation process to establish
and harmonize software upgrades across the Army.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-12. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-9
chunks.text:
Develop guidance based on Secretariat-promulgated policy for assistance in land acquisition and
military construction (MILCON) in support of the range and training land program in accordance with AR
420-1.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-12. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-9
chunks.text:
Establish the guidelines for base operations support (BASOPS)/sustainment, restoration, and mod-
ernization (SRM) MILCON support of installation and unit training strategies.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-12. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-9
chunks.text:
Plan, program, and budget BASOPS and/or SRM at Army installations to enable training strategies
and Soldier quality of life.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-12. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-9
chunks.text:
Identify unit set fielding (USF) facility/installation “pacing items.” In coordination with DCS, G-3/5/7,
DCS, G-8, and MATDEVs; prioritize and fund these items as part of unit set fielding integration reviews.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-12. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-9
chunks.text:
In coordination with U.S. Army Installation Management Command (IMCOM) and the U.S. Army
Corps of Engineers, provide support to gaining ACOMs and installations which are developing facilities,
training infrastructure, and other installation support requirements for USF.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-12. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-9
chunks.text:
Provide program oversight, guidance, and assistance for implementing installation agreements re-
lated to training and leader development.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-12. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-9
chunks.text: Through the CG, IMCOM-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-12. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-9
chunks.text:
Execute training support (range operations, integrated training area management (ITAM), training
support center, and Virtual and Constructive Simulations Center operations) within 48 states (CONUS):
RA and USAR installations. This will be consistent with the mission commander’s priorities.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-12. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-9
chunks.text: Support Civilian leader development (training and education) within the command.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-12. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-9
chunks.text:
Provide BASOPS/SRM to support the execution of institutional and unit training strategies by
ACOM mission commanders.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-12. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-9
chunks.text:
Provide garrison base realignment and closure plans and points of contact information to TRADOC
G-3/5/7 for digital training facility coordination.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-12. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-9
chunks.text:
Exercise responsibility on matters pertaining to installation readiness and family, morale, welfare,
and recreation programs.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-12. Deputy Chief of Staff, G-9
chunks.text:
Prepare requirements for and execute TSS Services on CONUS RA and USAR installations in ac-
cordance with AR 350-52.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text:
Informing the SECARMY and appropriate members of the Secretariat of all significant training mat-
ters and communications provided to others pertaining to NGB/ARNG.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text:
Prescribing the training, discipline, and requirements for the ARNG and allocating Federal funds for
the training of the ARNG. This includes developing an implementation plan to support the Army Leader
Development Strategy (ALDS) and place emphasis on leader development across all cohorts (officers,
warrant officers, NCOs, and Army Civilians).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text:
