Ensuring that units and members of the ARNG are trained by the States, Territories, and District of
Columbia, in accordance with approved policies and programs of the SECARMY.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text:
Monitoring and assisting the States, Territories, and the District of Columbia, consistent with
SECARMY or Secretary of Defense guidance, in the organization, maintenance and operation of ARNG
units to provide well-trained and well-equipped units capable of supporting and augmenting the total
Army, including conducting Homeland Defense and Defense Support of Civil Authorities missions.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text:
Planning, programming, and administering the budget for the ARNG and coordinating with the ASA
(M&RA) to ensure consistency with DA policy and total Army interests.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text:
Ensuring supervision of the Active Guard and Reserve program as it pertains to the ARNG in accord-
ance with processes and priorities established by the SECARMY.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text:
Ensuring Army officials and personnel of the NGB comply with applicable DA policies, issuances,
publications and legal opinions and that appointments of all Army officials and personnel of the NGB com-
ply with applicable Army personnel and manpower policies.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text:
Developing and promulgating policies, as authorized by the SECARMY in applicable Army direc-
tives, regulations and publications on ARNG matters consistent with DA policies.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text:
Facilitating and coordinating with other HQDA Principal Officials, including the ASA (M&RA); DCS,
G-1; and DCS, G-3/5/7 on the development of departmental policies and programs for mobilizing and
employing the ARNG as part of the Army's operational force to meet requirements.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text: Description of additional activities include the following:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text:
Ensuring ARNG units are organized, trained, and prepared for mobilization, deployment, and mis-
sions in support of the CCDR or as otherwise directed. Certify and approve pre-mobilization training in
accordance with current guidelines on RC training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text: Ensuring HQDA policies are used for training the ARNG.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text: Ensuring weapons qualification and training policies are used for ARNG.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text: Providing input for updates of Army maneuver and weapons training strategies.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text:
Programing and budgeting to support ARNG training requirements as defined by Army maneuver
and weapons training strategies.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text: Prescribing programs to maintain physical readiness of the ARNG.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text:
Developing procedures and standards for training programs in coordination with Army commands
(ACOMs), Army service component commands (ASCCs), and direct reporting unit (DRUs).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text: Assisting FORSCOM in the development of command training guidance as it pertains to the ARNG.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text:
Coordinating and facilitating the support of the appropriate ASCC for training support when re-
quired and requested.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text:
Coordinating with Army component commanders on the units participating in overseas deploy-
ment training (ODT).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text: Supporting the States, Territories, and District of Columbia in the training of ARNG units.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text:
Developing procedures and standards for training ARNG Active Guard Reserve (AGR) program
participants.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text:
Establishing military education prerequisites in appropriate ARNG regulations, except for criteria
prescribed in AR 135-155.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text: Recommending policy on professional career development of Soldiers in ARNG.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text:
Establishing initial active duty for training (IADT) requirements for MOS-producing Total Army
Training System (TATS) courses for the ARNG.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text: Representing ARNG in the structure and manning decision review (SMDR) process.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text:
Ensuring the selection of ARNG personnel to attend Army and DoD schools. Schedule ARNG of-
ficers for the BOLC, Captains Career Course (CCC), Warrant Officer Candidate School (WOCS), Warrant
Officer Basic course (WOBC), Warrant Officer Intermediate Course (WOIC), Warrant Officer Advanced
Course (WOAC), Warrant Officer Senior Course (WOSC), and Warrant Officer Master Course (WOMC).
State Adjutants General will control quotas and monitor input for resident NCOPDS courses conducted by
TASS institutions.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text:
Supporting peacetime training requirements for ARNG Soldiers in Total Army Training System
(TATS) courses.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text: Ensuring the management of all NGB personnel attending non-Army schools in ATRRS.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text:
Developing, in coordination with the ACOMs, policy for access, use, and management of ATRRS
in ARNG.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text: Establishing and providing guidance and direction to ARNG TASS organizations.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text: Programing and budgeting to support TASS.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text:
Supporting compliance with the DAWIA and DoD-directed acquisition position qualifications and
certification requirements.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text:
Ensuring ARNG participation in the new equipment training (NET) and displaced equipment train-
ing (DET) planning process to include a review of the training strategy as outlined in the system training
plan (STRAP).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text:
Providing DCS, G-3/5/7 with a quarterly courseware development report. The report is due on the
tenth working day following the end of each quarter.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text:
Ensuring the development, validation, and maintenance of ARNG training requirements in ATRRS
for DoD and Army schools, and training battalions under TASS.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text:
Ensuring the review of annually current institutional training force structure requirements (instructors,
support personnel, and so forth) to determine the right structure to better support the institutional training
mission in coordination with TRADOC, USARC and HQDA. Document enduring requirements.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text:
Accounting for the total institutional training instructor and support personnel requirements for NGB,
in coordination with DCS, G-3/5/7.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text:
Participating in the Training Coordination Council Workshop (TCCW). Identify ARNG constraints in
manpower, infrastructure, services, and equipment to TRADOC.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text:
Ensuring the execution of training support (range operations, integrated training area management,
training support center, supporting culminating events, lane support and mission training complex (MTC)
operations) at ARNG installations, consistent with the mission commander’s priorities.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text: Ensuring preparation requirements for, and execution of TSS Services on ARNG installations.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text:
Ensuring subordinate commands/Soldiers are aware of and understand the Institutional Training Di-
rected Lodging and Meal (ITDLM) Program and authorized/approved Institutional Training travel entitle-
ments.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text:
Ensuring the issuance of ARNG training support funds to USACE and AMC through General Fund
Enterprise Business System (GFEBS) with instructions to use the funds to support ARNG resident stu-
dent lodging and directed meal requirements under the ITDLM Program.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text:
Ensuring the development of an implementation plan to meet the intent of and support the ALDS and
place emphasis on leader development across all cohorts (officers, warrant officers, noncommissioned
officers, and Army Civilians).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-13. Chief, National Guard Bureau
chunks.text:
Ensuring DCS, G-3/5/7 (Training Directorate) approval on proposed changes, which result in re-
source increases, such as: new courses, course length, class size and changes to prerequisites, causing
additional resources for courses provided by ARNG schools.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-14. Chief, Army Reserve
chunks.text:
Serve dual-hatted as CAR and CG, USARC. In their role as CG, USARC, execute training responsi-
bilities as a major subordinate commander of FORSCOM in accordance with paragraph 2-20l.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-14. Chief, Army Reserve
chunks.text:
Serve as the HQDA advisor on USAR training and leader development matters, and coordinate
such with the ASCCs with USAR training responsibilities (for example, USAREUR and USARPAC).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-14. Chief, Army Reserve
chunks.text:
Ensure USAR units are organized, trained, and prepared for mobilization and commitment to war
and other missions in support of the CCDR or as otherwise directed in accordance with this regulation
and AR 10-87.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-14. Chief, Army Reserve
chunks.text:
Implement, review, and provide supplemental guidance on any DoD, HQDA, and
ACOM/ASCC/DRU policies for organization, operations, training, mobilization, and deployment of USAR
forces.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-14. Chief, Army Reserve
chunks.text:
Review and provide input for updates to Army training strategies which include capabilities and
needs of USAR forces. Strategies include training policies/resourcing of training readiness, Standard
METL, ARTEP, CATS, weapons qualification, physical fitness, TSS, Distributed Learning System, and
Army Training Information System.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-14. Chief, Army Reserve
chunks.text:
Review and provide input for updates to ARTEP Products and Standard METL. Participate in the
STRAG METL approval process.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-14. Chief, Army Reserve
chunks.text:
Plan, program, budget, and execute USAR appropriations (that is, reserve pay & allowances, opera-
tions & maintenance, USAR, military construction, Army Reserve, National Guard Reserve equipment ac-
count) to support requirements of the Army Reserve Training Strategy.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-14. Chief, Army Reserve
chunks.text: Represent the USAR in the ALDP.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-14. Chief, Army Reserve
chunks.text: Review all STRAPS.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-14. Chief, Army Reserve
chunks.text:
Establish priorities to support training requirements for USAR units and execute training support (the
sustainable range program, training support center, MTC, and LVC integrated training environment IT
(ITE) operations) at USAR funded installations (Fort McCoy, WI; Fort Hunter Liggett, CA; ASA-Dix, NJ;
Devens RFTA, MA, Fort Buchanan, PR; and Camp Parks, CA) and local training areas. Develop policy,
procedures, and standards for training USAR AGR Soldiers.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-14. Chief, Army Reserve
chunks.text: Conduct selection boards for professional development courses for USAR Soldiers.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-14. Chief, Army Reserve
chunks.text:
Participate in the SMDR, Total Army Centralized Individual Training Solicitation (TACITS), and
TCCW in accordance with AR 350-10. Identify USAR institutional training needs (for example, individual
and leader education training requirements). Identify TASS support constraints in manpower, infrastruc-
ture, services, and equipment to TRADOC.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-14. Chief, Army Reserve
chunks.text:
Review and provide input for updates to ARTEP Products and Standards METL. Participate in the
STRAG METL approval process.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-14. Chief, Army Reserve
chunks.text:
Develop, in coordination with the ACOMs, ASCCs, and/or DRUs, policy for access, use, and man-
agement of ATRRS for USAR forces.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-14. Chief, Army Reserve
chunks.text: Coordinate with ASCC for overseas deployment training (ODT).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-14. Chief, Army Reserve
chunks.text: Recommend policy on professional career development of Soldiers in the USAR.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-14. Chief, Army Reserve
chunks.text:
Establish policy and procedures for the development, validation, and maintenance of the USAR non-
prior service and in-service individual training requirements.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-14. Chief, Army Reserve
chunks.text:
Ensure attendance of qualified officers, NCOs, and Soldiers at OES and NCOPDS courses, are con-
sistent with Army training policies and priorities.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-14. Chief, Army Reserve
chunks.text:
Determine and document institutional training force structure requirements (for example, instructors,
support personnel, equipment, TADSS, MDEPs) to support the institutional training mission.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-14. Chief, Army Reserve
chunks.text:
Assist with TSS planning and monitor USAR unit use of IMCOM executed training enabler resources
that support senior commander-directed training programs.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-14. Chief, Army Reserve
chunks.text:
Develop an implementation plan to meet the intent of and support the ALDS with emphasis on
leader development across all cohorts (officers, warrant officers, noncommissioned officers, and Army
Civilians).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-14. Chief, Army Reserve
chunks.text:
Ensure subordinate commands/Soldiers are aware of and understand the ITDLM Program and au-
thorized/approved Institutional Training travel entitlements.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-14. Chief, Army Reserve
chunks.text:
Issue USAR training support funds to USACE and AMC through GFEBS with instructions to use the
funds to support USAR resident student lodging and directed meal requirements under the ITDLM Pro-
gram.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-15. The Surgeon General
chunks.text:
Exercise HQDA responsibility for health and medical training and identify strategies and resources
for health and medical training. This includes medical training for individuals and units not in the AMEDD
and medical training under special circumstances, such as chemical, biological, radiological, nuclear
(CBRN), and explosive hazard conditions.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-15. The Surgeon General
chunks.text:
Exercise HQDA responsibility for functional aspects of officer, warrant officer, enlisted, and Army
Civilian medical specialist training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-15. The Surgeon General
chunks.text:
Provide technical advice and assistance to the Army Staff pertaining to the health and medical as-
pects of manning, training and equipping the Army, and for matters on public health, readiness of the
force, warrior transition care, medical force structure and equipping, force development, medical materiel
and research and development, medical evacuation, and medical military construction.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-15. The Surgeon General
chunks.text:
Develop and coordinate the Army’s position on Joint medical training. Monitor, evaluate, and provide
guidance for improving the status of medical training readiness as reflected in unit status reports for
AMEDD units.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-15. The Surgeon General
chunks.text:
Exercise HQDA responsibility for combat lifesaver and casualty response training, medical intelli-
gence training, and medical mobilization training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-15. The Surgeon General
chunks.text: Provide guidance for medical, physiological, and health areas related to H2F/physical readiness.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-15. The Surgeon General
chunks.text:
Develop, coordinate, and monitor programs for training foreign nationals in AMEDD schools and fa-
cilities.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-15. The Surgeon General
chunks.text: Maintain HQDA supervision and plan, program, and budget for NET for medical materiel.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-15. The Surgeon General
chunks.text:
Approve Army doctrinal qualification, personnel policy, policies pertaining to AMEDD professional
qualification, and AMEDD-specific equipment requirements, in accordance with AR 5-22.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-15. The Surgeon General
chunks.text:
Develop and direct the Army’s planning, programming, budgeting and execution (PPBE) process for
Defense Health Program requirements to support medical training and readiness, in accordance with AR

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 1-1.
chunks.text:
Coordinate with AFC and TRADOC (MEDCoE) to develop Army Health System support concepts,
doctrine, training, education and user tests.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-16. The Judge Advocate General
chunks.text:
Develop and administer the curriculum, doctrine, standards, and delivery of legal education and
training for attorneys (military and Army Civilian), warrant officers, legal paraprofessionals, paralegal
NCOs, and specialists in the Judge Advocate Legal Service and provide input on training requirements to
the DCS, G-1 in support of the SMDR and TRAP processes.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-16. The Judge Advocate General
chunks.text:
Advise DCS, G-3/5/7 (Training Directorate); DCS, G-1, and CG, TRADOC, during the development
of training and training support products for the Army including training programs mandated by domestic
and international law obligations.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-16. The Judge Advocate General
chunks.text: Develop training support products for Armywide legal training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-16. The Judge Advocate General
chunks.text:
Review and provide input for updates to ARTEP Products and Standards METL. Participate in the
STRAG METL approval process.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-16. The Judge Advocate General
chunks.text:
Assign JAGC officers to Direct Commission Course, Judge Advocate Officer Basic Course and ei-
ther the Judge Advocate Officer’s Graduate Course or the Reserve Judge Advocate Officer’s Advanced
Course.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-16. The Judge Advocate General
chunks.text: Exercise HQDA responsibility for training on the law of war.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-16. The Judge Advocate General
chunks.text: Administer the Funded Legal Education Program in accordance with AR 27-1.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-16. The Judge Advocate General
chunks.text: Establish professional and educational policies and procedures for the JAGC.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-16. The Judge Advocate General
chunks.text: Direct education, training and functions of The Judge Advocate General’s Legal Center and School.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-16. The Judge Advocate General
chunks.text: Program and budget resources to meet requirements.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-17. Chief of Chaplains
chunks.text:
Develop and publish proponent Army learning strategy that guides implementation of training, edu-
cation, and self-learning and the three areas of Army learning for the Chaplain Corps.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-17. Chief of Chaplains
chunks.text: Develop and publish annual training and leader development guidance for the Chaplain Corps.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-17. Chief of Chaplains
chunks.text: Develop professional and educational policies and procedures for the Chaplain Corps.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-17. Chief of Chaplains
chunks.text: Establish chaplain career training and professional development objectives.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-17. Chief of Chaplains
chunks.text:
Provide professional training for chaplains, religious affairs specialists, chaplain candidates and di-
rectors of religious education.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-17. Chief of Chaplains
chunks.text: Select and schedule active-duty chaplains to attend Army and non-Army schools.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-17. Chief of Chaplains
chunks.text:
Assign active-duty chaplains to attend the Chaplain Basic Officer Leader Course, the Chaplains
Captains Career Course (C4), and branch functional courses.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-17. Chief of Chaplains
chunks.text: Direct training and function of the U.S. Army Institute for Religious Leadership.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-17. Chief of Chaplains
chunks.text: Direct the training and function of the U.S. Army Family Life Chaplain Training Centers.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-17. Chief of Chaplains
chunks.text: Program and budget resources to meet training requirements.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-17. Chief of Chaplains
chunks.text:
Ensure that the Chaplain Corps is trained to provide religious support across the full range of military
operations.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-17. Chief of Chaplains
chunks.text: Ensure seamless integration of religious support doctrine and training throughout the Army.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-17. Chief of Chaplains
chunks.text:
Develop and provide training at selected Army schools on topics to include ethics, world religions,
moral leadership, spiritual readiness, and Soldier and Family readiness.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-17. Chief of Chaplains
chunks.text:
Provide specialized training to chaplains in the following areas: Resource Management, and Logis-
tics; Family Life Ministry; Family Life Ministry Supervisor; Clinical Pastoral Education; Clinical Pastoral Ed-
ucation Supervisor; Chaplain Clinical Ethicist; Ethics; World Religions; and relationship resilience.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-17. Chief of Chaplains
chunks.text: Exercise HQDA responsibility for spiritual readiness and moral leadership in the Army.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-18. Provost Marshal General and U.S. Army Criminal Investigation Division
chunks.text:
Develop respective policies and provide management and technical oversight of the Army’s policing
and felony-level criminal investigative functions, including law enforcement, criminal investigations, crimi-
nal intelligence fusion, corrections, forensics, physical security, high risk personnel security, AT and de-
tention operations.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-18. Provost Marshal General and U.S. Army Criminal Investigation Division
chunks.text:
Serve as the responsible official to the SECARMY’s Department of Defense Executive Agent re-
sponsibilities for Forensics, Level III Corrections, Detainee Operations policy, eGuardian Suspicious Ac-
tivity Reporting System and Civilian Police and Guards Training and Fitness Standards. Serve the Secre-
tary of the Army (Executive Agent) as the Responsible Official and Executive Manager for DoD Biomet-
rics.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-19. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct
chunks.text: Train and evaluate assigned forces in accordance with this regulation, AR 10-87 and AR 140-1.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-19. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct
chunks.text:
Develop an implementation plan to support the ALDS and place emphasis on leader development
across all cohorts (officers, warrant officers, noncommissioned officers, and Army Civilians).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-19. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct
chunks.text: Manage NCOPDS courses taught in regional NCO academies (NCOAs).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-19. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct
chunks.text: Program and budget resources for operation of the basic level NCOPDS course as applicable.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-19. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct
chunks.text:
Ensure attendance of qualified officers, NCOs, and Soldiers at OES and NCOPDS courses, con-
sistent with Army training policies and priorities.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-19. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct
chunks.text: Manage training quotas allocated to their command, and Soldier attendance.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-19. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct
chunks.text: Manage Basic Leader Course (BLC) training seats and attendance.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-19. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct
chunks.text: Select Soldiers to attend the BLC in accordance with the policies established in this regulation.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-19. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct
chunks.text:
Program and budget for associated temporary duty travel costs related to attendance to BLC, ALC,
SLC, and functional training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-19. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct
chunks.text:
Ensure Army Civilians attend grade level specific Civilian Education System (CES) education at the
time of selection in accordance with policies established in this regulation.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-19. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct
chunks.text:
Support compliance with Defense Acquisition Workforce Improvement Act and DoD-directed acqui-
sition position qualifications and certification requirements.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-19. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct
chunks.text: Ensure when commanders receive new, improved, or modified equipment-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-19. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct
chunks.text:
Develop facility, training support infrastructure, training support, and management support resource
requirements to execute USF.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-19. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct
chunks.text: Review, update, and provide input to the NET Plans.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-19. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct
chunks.text:
Assist the MATDEV and TNGDEV to determine prerequisites (MOS, skills, and grade) for attending
training conducted by the new equipment training team (NETT).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-19. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct
chunks.text:
Augment, as appropriate, NETT with personnel when beyond MATDEVs or provider’s capability (as
identified and agreed to in NET Plan), and request availability and stabilization of personnel receiving
NET and designated NETT members in support of the NET Plan.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-19. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct
chunks.text:
Provide installation support to include billeting, tactical vehicle transportation, and food services to
NETT as available and required on a reimbursable basis.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-19. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct
chunks.text: Ensure personnel receiving training from NETT meet the requirements of DA Pam 600-8.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-19. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct
chunks.text: Provide an after-action report to MATDEV after completion of training by NETT.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-19. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct
chunks.text:
Assist capability developer (CAPDEV) in preparing combat developments feedback data and assist
MATDEV and TNGDEV in preparing DET plans, as required.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-19. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct
chunks.text: Notify HRC of any MOS or ASI awarded because of AMT.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-19. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct
chunks.text:
Program, budget, fund travel and per diem for ACOM personnel to attend NET courses (except
NET team members) and provide DET information to CAR.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-19. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct
chunks.text:
Manage DET for USAR units, as required, in conjunction with MATDEV and TNGDEV. Assist
ARNG units receiving DET, when required.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-19. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct
chunks.text:
Commands will support the Army’s QA mission in evaluating and assessing the quality of the insti-
tutional Army’s ability to meet the needs of the operational Army. The Army QA program provides over-
sight of doctrine, training and education provided by all Army schools (TRADOC and non-TRADOC, mili-
tary and civilian, RA and RC). ASCCs and DRUs will identify appropriate organizations or points of con-
tact for coordination/ oversight of institutional accreditation as appropriate.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-19. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct
chunks.text:
Review and provide input for updates to ARTEP Products and Standards METL. Participate in the
STRAG METL approval process.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-19. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct
chunks.text:
Ensure that if the command is the training proponent for a Soldier training course (see para and app
C), or otherwise designated by DCS, G-3/5/7-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-19. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct
chunks.text:
Develop TSPs for the course and, if requested, provide the TSP to other ACOMs. Development of
course TSPs will follow the guidance published in appropriate TRADOC publications. TSPs will include
standardized course prerequisites and standardized certification tests. The ACOM points of contact can
secure information about TSPs developed by proponent ACOMs and Training courseware development
from HQ, TRADOC.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-19. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct
chunks.text:
Ensure installations conduct an annual needs assessment to validate the continuing need for local
Soldier training courses. When requested by DCS, G-3/5/7 (Training Directorate), summarize the needs
assessment for each training course contracted by subordinate installations, including existing and pro-
posed new courses.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-19. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct
chunks.text:
Provide, when requested, DCS, G-3/5/7 (Training Directorate), a projection of Soldier training
course expenditures for the next fiscal year.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-19. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct
chunks.text: Incorporate, as applicable, ITDLM Program requirements in Command Inspection Program.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-19. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct
chunks.text:
Develop an implementation plan to meet the intent of and support the ALDS and place emphasis on
leader development across all cohorts (officers, warrant officers, noncommissioned officers, and Army
Civilians).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-19. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct
chunks.text: Support Army Civilian leader development (training and education) within the command.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-19. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct
chunks.text:
For any pre-deployment training in support of respective CCDR mission requirements beyond LSCO
(like-type-units with standard unit METL) requests-for-forces, minimum expectations include: identification
of all tasks (individual and collective) pre-deployment training requirements by an Army Task Numeric,
task title, task description, doctrinal reference, and supported by a Training and Evaluation Outline in the
Combined Arms Training Strategies (CATS) or training support package if appropriate.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Commanding General, U.S. Army Forces Command
chunks.text:
Serve as the Army’s responsible official to coordinate and synchronize force generation (see AR
525-29).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Commanding General, U.S. Army Forces Command
chunks.text: Train and evaluate Army forces in accordance with this regulation, AR 10-87, and AR 140-1.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Commanding General, U.S. Army Forces Command
chunks.text:
Serve as the force provider for Army conventional forces and, as such, will execute the functions,
duties, and responsibilities of a Service Force Provider as required by the Secretary of Defense. Specific
to the Service Force Provider mission (see AR 10-87), the SECARMY delegated to FORSCOM the train-
ing and readiness oversight responsibility over RC conventional forces in the continental United States
(CONUS) that includes Puerto Rico and U.S. Virgin Islands, which are not assigned to a CCMD.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Commanding General, U.S. Army Forces Command
chunks.text:
Train, mobilize, deploy, sustain, transform and reconstitute assigned conventional forces to meet the
needs of CCDRs. Validate that assigned units are prepared for deployment.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Commanding General, U.S. Army Forces Command
chunks.text:
Provide input and assistance as needed to update the Army’s maneuver and weapons training strat-
egies.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Commanding General, U.S. Army Forces Command
chunks.text: Responsible for the NTC and the JRTC. Man NTC/JRTC observer, coach, trainer requirements.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Commanding General, U.S. Army Forces Command
chunks.text: Exercise responsibility for NTC, JRTC, and MCTP scheduling.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Commanding General, U.S. Army Forces Command
chunks.text:
Review and provide input for updates to ARTEP Products and Standard METL. Participate in the
STRAG approval process.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Commanding General, U.S. Army Forces Command
chunks.text: As the Army lead agent, coordinate and synchronize unit-level LSCO training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Commanding General, U.S. Army Forces Command
chunks.text:
Exercise responsibility for the CONUS Joint Airborne/Air Transportability Training (JA/ATT) Pro-
gram (see app E).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Commanding General, U.S. Army Forces Command
chunks.text:
Advocate per Joint Staff directed timelines and process for all unified action partner capabilities for
Home Station Training, CTC and other Army sponsored exercises, tests, and experiments (Network Inte-
gration) (see CJCSI 3500.01H).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Commanding General, U.S. Army Forces Command
chunks.text:
Assess Security Assistance Force and Joint Task Force-North/ARNORTH requests for unified ac-
tion partner training value and collaborate with U.S. Army Security Assistance Command to leverage
these events as METL relevant training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Commanding General, U.S. Army Forces Command
chunks.text: Provide equipment support for training programs at TASS organizations, as requested and available.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Commanding General, U.S. Army Forces Command
chunks.text: Provide trained units to develop and test Army organization, equipment, and doctrine.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Commanding General, U.S. Army Forces Command
chunks.text:
Assist with TSS planning and monitor FORSCOM unit use of IMCOM executed training enabler re-
sources that support senior commander directed training programs.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Commanding General, U.S. Army Forces Command
chunks.text: The CG, USARC-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Commanding General, U.S. Army Forces Command
chunks.text:
Command USAR troop program units (TPUs) and reinforcement training units in CONUS other
than forces assigned in 10 USC 167 (unified combatant command for special operations forces).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Commanding General, U.S. Army Forces Command
chunks.text:
Ensure USARC units are organized, trained, and prepared for mobilization, commitment to war,
and other missions in support of the CCDR or as otherwise directed.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Commanding General, U.S. Army Forces Command
chunks.text: Publish command training guidance which addresses USAR forces.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Commanding General, U.S. Army Forces Command
chunks.text:
Implement, review, and provide supplemental guidance on any DoD, HQDA, and
ACOM/ASCC/DRU policies for operations, training, mobilization, and deployment of USARC forces.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Commanding General, U.S. Army Forces Command
chunks.text: Manage the combat support training program (CSTP).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Commanding General, U.S. Army Forces Command
chunks.text: Participate in the TACITS, SMDR, and TRAP in accordance with AR 350-10.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Commanding General, U.S. Army Forces Command
chunks.text: Provide command and control for Troop Schools in FORSCOM.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Commanding General, U.S. Army Forces Command
chunks.text:
Review the relevance of the AWT in support of theater operations, annually, in coordination with
TRADOC and the USAIS.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Commanding General, U.S. Army Forces Command
chunks.text:
Execute the Army’s Parent Service Validation responsibilities for deploying CONUS conventional
ARNG and USAR units, as HQDA responsible official.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Commanding General, U.S. Army Forces Command
chunks.text:
Support, upon request, the training of ARNG/USAR units in CONUS in accordance with current
guidance on RC training. CG FORSCOM, in coordination with RC components, will develop processes
and procedures to prioritize, schedule, coordinate and implement RA pre-mobilization training support to
RC units.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Commanding General, U.S. Army Forces Command
chunks.text: Establish criteria and oversee/validate pre-deployment training of ARNG/USAR units in CONUS.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text: Administer training functions outlined in this regulation and AR 10-87.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Serve as the Army SRO and principal lead for the training, education, and leader development pro-
cesses. Develop training doctrine and execute training policies and procedures as directed by HQDA.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Designate Army training and education proponents from among TRADOC-assigned force moderni-
zation and branch proponents responsible for the development of unit and individual training and the de-
velopment of TSPs and products for approved training requirements.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Serve as the Army’s lead for training and education development products and processes applica-
ble to all Army training and education proponents (TRADOC and non-TRADOC, active and reserve, mili-
tary and civilian) except for the U.S. Military Academy.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Develop, publish, and sustain training management doctrinal products (see ADP 7-0 and FM 7-0),
supporting pamphlets, and web access (ATN).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text: Develop and implement TASS policies and procedures.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Develop and update an ALDS based on guidance from DA senior leaders; SA; ASA (M&RA); ASA
(FMC); CSA; VCSA; Sergeant Major of the Army (SMA); DCS, G-1; DCS, G-3/5/7; DCS, G-8; and other
HQDA principals, as required.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Assess the ALDP and related policies to ensure they are consistent with the Army Leader Develop-
ment Strategy.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text: Lead, shape, manage and execute leader development for the Army.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Serve as the Army lead agent for Army training information systems and ensure continuity in training
management (unit and institution), DL and TSS from legacy to future capabilities; and all system inter-
faces as required.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Serve as the Army lead agent for DL implementation and provide oversight for DL implementation
and integration throughout the life of the program. Coordinate the DL training requirements with DCS, G-1
to ensure ATRRS entries as required.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Develop, promulgate, and implement procedures to provide for quality control and quality assurance
of Army training doctrine development, training products, and training management programs by-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Serving as the Army functional proponent for training management, doctrinal products (see
ADP/FM 7-0), ATMS, Army Training Network, CATS, Standard METL, and DTMS. In coordination with
DCS, G-3/5/7, G-37 (Collective Training Division) exercise HQDA responsibility, supervision, and man-
agement of ATMS providing, technical, functional, and operational support to the Army and DoD. Ensure
that doctrinal products (ADP/FM 7-0, ATMS Army Training Network, ARTEP, CATS, METL, DTMS) are
maintained, periodically reevaluated, synchronized and comply with and implement the guidance pro-
vided in this regulation.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Developing policy and guidance for the management, planning, development, and implementation
of collective training and for preparing collective training products. In coordination with DCS, G-3/5/7, G-
37 collective training division exercise HQDA supervision and management of the Army Training and
Evaluation Program (ARTEP) for developing policy and guidance for the management, planning, develop-
ment, and implementation of collective training and for preparing collective training products.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Identifying and prioritizing training resources required by ATMS, Army Training Network, DTMS,
CATS, and METL.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Serving as the Army’s task (collective and individual), CATS, ARTEP, and Standard METL man-
ager.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Serve as an Army Organizational Point of Contract (OPOC) for the Universal Joint Task List
(UJTL). Maintain the Army Universal Task List (AUTL) as the Army's Service Task List and provide to JS
J-7.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Exercising quality assurance and quality control over training development and collective training
products.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text: Developing related individual TSPs for individual training in service schools and units.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text: Supporting the evolution of the Joint Training System and its automation.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text: Developing training strategies for the Army by:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Developing maneuver and weapons training strategies (CATS and DA Pam 350-38) for training in
units and programs of instruction.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text: Identifying training resources required by those strategies.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Manage the enlisted, warrant officer, and officer individual training and military education programs,
including courseware, training support products, and TADSS.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Design, develop, and distribute military and Civilian training and education programs and products in
support of individual and unit training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Integrate Army leadership doctrine in all training programs of the schools and training centers for of-
ficers, warrant officers, noncommissioned officers, and Army Civilians at progressive and sequential
phases of career development.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text: Ensure TNGDEV for AMT-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Works in concert with the other CAPDEV and MATDEV to identify and document training and train-
ing support product requirements.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Validates all the training products provided by the MATDEVs and TNGDEVs prior to the first unit
equipped (FUE) date by the TRADOC proponent.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Documents the AMT strategy and the required associated NET and training support products to
provide the TRADOC proponent with the necessary material so the Generating Force is prepared to im-
plement the new training into the Operational Force by FUE, with complete transition not later than 1 year
after FUE.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Enables Army training activities by ensuring training requirements are identified in the appropriate
Joint Capabilities Integration and Development System (JCIDS) capability requirement documents.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Ensures NET and multimedia TSPs are provided in a timely manner to units conducting AWEs and
advanced technology demonstrations in coordination with DCS, G-3/5/7 (Training Directorate), and
MATDEV or provider.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Identifies requirements for TADSS to be fielded to the training base prior to the start of a desig-
nated USF window.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Identifies institutional training requirements to support USF modernization/modularization training
and submit requirements as part of the POM build.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Begins the institutional training portion of AMT no later than 12 months after FUE. Exceptions to
this policy include systems fielded in such low density that institutional training is not warranted, and NET
or unit sustainment TSP will satisfy the training needed for the life of the system; or a system that is
fielded in such low densities over an extended fielding schedule that it does not warrant beginning institu-
tional training until a significant density level is achieved. However, institutional training timelines must be
responsive to all acquisition pathways.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text: Assists MATDEV or provider in preparing and updating NET Plans.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Determines ammunition requirements to support AMT in coordination with MATDEV for forecast-
ing by the gaining commands.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text: Assesses AMT effectiveness.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Establishes mobile training teams or provide DL remedial instruction when approved by DCS, G-
3/5/7 (Training Directorate).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text: Refers the unresolved AMT issues to DCS, G-3/5/7 (Training Directorate).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Exercise responsibility for the capability development and training development aspects of the oper-
ational environment and opposing forces (OPFOR) Program in accordance with AR 350-2.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Assist HQDA with the management of TADSS requirements documentation, TADSS assets, the
graphic training aid program, TADLP, DA Pam 350-38, range modernization and standardization, interna-
tional military training and education under the Security Assistance Training Program, training standardi-
zation policy, and TASS.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Maintain the capability to advise DCS, G-3/5/7 on all matters regarding physical readiness re-
search, policy recommendations, performance standards and to assist in the execution of the Army Holis-
tic Health and Fitness (H2F) System in units, training base schools, and mobilization sites to include
physical readiness for individuals/units, Future Soldier, initial-entry, pregnancy/post-partum, combatives,
and water survival. The Commanding General, U.S. Army Center for Initial Military Training (USACIMT),
as the specified proponent (AR 5-22) for H2F, provides guidance concerning theories and principles of
human performance and exercise science as informed by empirical research, institutional best practices,
and experiential learning to drive H2F doctrine including periodic individual/unit training programs and
performance standards and testing.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Assist the DCS, G-3/5/7 (Director of Training), to develop, field, and sustain the capability to rapidly
adapt training development and training management systems (for example ATMS).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text: Maintain administrative control over designated DoD schools.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Exercise responsibility and manage Army training for foreign military and Civilian personnel in the
United States.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text: Serve as the Army’s individual and collective training task manager and-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Receive external training requirements and conduct training needs analyses to determine applica-
bility for individual and collective training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Designate Army training proponents responsible to develop TSPs and products for approved indi-
vidual and collective training requirements.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Manage the development of vertically and horizontally aligned officer (to include pre-commission-
ing), warrant officer, and noncommissioned officer training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text: Manage common Soldier shared and branch-specific individual and collective tasks.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Review the relevance of the AWT Program in support of decisive actions, biennially, in coordination
with FORSCOM; TRADOC Deputy Commanding General (DCG) Initial Entry Training (IET); and the Ma-
neuver CoE. Provide storage of, access to, and distribution of common task information resources.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Include common tasks in officer (to include pre-commissioning), warrant officer, noncommissioned
officer, and Army Civilian training, as appropriate.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Ensure the application of common task training throughout TASS to include the coordination of pre-
commissioning tasks taught by the USMA and National Guard Academies.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Identify and notify DCS, G-3/5/7 (Training Directorate), of the impact of resource deficiencies on
unit mission and task performance accomplishment.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Obtain DCS, G-3/5/7 (Training Directorate) approval in cases where a course change results in a
change of status from temporary duty (TDY) to permanent change of station (PCS), or vice versa.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Obtain DCS, G-3/5/7 (Training Directorate) approval for course deletions that will have Armywide
implications or when classes are cancelled when a certified instructor or essential equipment is not avail-
able.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Establish institutional military leader training courses that provide training in leadership, technical,
and tactical tasks relevant to branch missions for the type units students will serve in after training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Develop and publish guidance for the development of training products and training support prod-
ucts.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text: Coordinate pre-commissioning military training among pre-commissioning sources.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Consult with DARNG and CAR on scheduling courses that are conducted when the preponderance
of students is ARNG or USAR.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Command and manage federal OCS, ROTC, and NDCC activities except those functions and re-
sponsibilities retained by HQDA.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Exercise administrative responsibility to- supervise, manage, operate, and fund the Defense Lan-
guage Institute Foreign Language Center (DLIFLC). Ensure AIT for language-dependent occupational
specialties includes requirements for demonstrating linguistic proficiency. Validate DLIFLC resources.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Supervise the development and implementation of an individual military training integration and
standardization program.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-21. Commanding General, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
Develop school mobilization missions and supporting policy and guidance.
aa. Maintain and distribute career development models in coordination with each career management
field proponent.
bb. Serve as the POI proponent for instructional programs at TASS training battalions.
cc. Through the CG, Combined Arms Center, serve as the proponent for mission command current
and emerging systems and technology.
dd. Ensure the Army fraternization policy contained in AR 600-32 is addressed in IET, NCOPDS, and
OES, as appropriate. Coordinate through OTJAG.
ee. Research and develop the parameters of the operational environment (including the training condi-
tions) necessary for appropriate training of Soldiers, leaders, and units in all training domains. Document
the operational environment for training in a periodically updated pamphlet for use by trainers in establish-
ing training conditions.
ff. Establish a special staff HQ, TRADOC Quality Assurance (QA) Office, as the lead agent for the
Army’s QA program, to-develop the responsibilities, policies, procedures, and objectives for the Army’s
QA program in coordination with other commands and stakeholders. Develop and maintain the policy,
guidance, and accreditation standards for the evaluation and assessment of Army learning institutions
across the DOTMLPF-P domains. Serve as the granting authority for Army accreditation for all Army
learning institutions (TRADOC and non-TRADOC, active and reserve, military and Civilian) except for the
U.S. Military Academy. Learning institutions will assign a QAO/QAE that is assigned and aligned directly
to an Army learning institutions CG, DCG, Commandant, or Commander. Oversee all proponent assess-
ments, serve as the lead agency for external evaluations, and develop and maintain a QA Program web
site for Army accreditation data sharing across all Army learning institutions.
gg. Chair the TCCW.
hh. Assist the TSS planning and monitor TRADOC unit use of IMCOM executed training enabler re-
sources that support Senior Commander directed training programs.
ii. Identify requirements for and manage execution of USARC Mission Training Complexes under the
command of the 84th Training Command.
jj. Support Civilian leader development (training and education) within the command.
kk. Develop the necessary controls to ensure all subordinate activities comply with guidance in the
ITDLM Handbook. Incorporate, as applicable, ITDLM program requirements in command inspection pro-
gram.
ll. Ensure DTMS provides necessary data to Defense Readiness Reporting System- Army (DRRS-A)
in support of commander’s unit status reporting.
mm. Ensure all PME schools adhere to the 365-postpartum body composition exemption for all eligible
PME attendees.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Commanding General, U.S. Army Materiel Command
chunks.text: Establish training functions as specified in this regulation and AR 10-87.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Commanding General, U.S. Army Materiel Command
chunks.text: Establish policy for and manage the Defense Ammunition Center.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Commanding General, U.S. Army Materiel Command
chunks.text:
Provide management training in industrial and business activities for the Wholesale Logistics Sys-
tem.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Commanding General, U.S. Army Materiel Command
chunks.text: Function as the quota manager for Defense Management Education and Training Program Courses.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Commanding General, U.S. Army Materiel Command
chunks.text:
Coordinate with DCS, G-2; PEO/PM U.S. Army Intelligence CoE; INSCOM and HQ, TRADOC to
evaluate cryptology and signal intelligence, measurements intelligence, and human intelligence
(HUMINT) training requirements related to tactical cryptology and national signal intelligence systems
(when the Army is designated as the system developer).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Commanding General, U.S. Army Materiel Command
chunks.text:
Ensure, through assigned NET managers, and in coordination with, program and systems manag-
ers-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Commanding General, U.S. Army Materiel Command
chunks.text:
Production of new equipment training plan (NETP) for each new, improved, or modified materiel
system based upon the approved capability requirement document. Coordinate NETP with the TNGDEV,
CAPDEV, gaining commands, test agencies, and other agencies, as required.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Commanding General, U.S. Army Materiel Command
chunks.text:
Coordination with HQDA to ensure visibility of funds for the acquisition of the system’s training sub-
system.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Commanding General, U.S. Army Materiel Command
chunks.text:
Resource and update TADSS and TSPs to keep pace with the system engineering change pro-
posals, technology insertions, and block improvements applied to the materiel system.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Commanding General, U.S. Army Materiel Command
chunks.text:
Validation of system TSPs, to include system TADSS and embedded training, are available to sup-
port FUE.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Commanding General, U.S. Army Materiel Command
chunks.text: PM identifies ammunition and consumables necessary to support AMT.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Commanding General, U.S. Army Materiel Command
chunks.text: Review of AMT ammunition requirements semiannually with PM.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Commanding General, U.S. Army Materiel Command
chunks.text:
Provide training for multiservice systems or equipment when the Army is designated as the NET
manager. Provide the training upon receipt of a statement of requirements and funding.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Commanding General, U.S. Army Materiel Command
chunks.text:
Identify training needs and ensure the lead military department includes the requirement in the ap-
propriate training plan, in coordination with the Army TNGDEV.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Commanding General, U.S. Army Materiel Command
chunks.text:
Program and budget the Army portion of NET unless the lead military department is required to
fund.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Commanding General, U.S. Army Materiel Command
chunks.text:
Assist TNGDEV in identifying MOS and AOC affected by NET. List the affected MOS/AOC in the
appropriate section of NETP.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Commanding General, U.S. Army Materiel Command
chunks.text:
Submit rosters of personnel who received NET directly to Commander, HRC, in coordination with
the gaining command.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Commanding General, U.S. Army Materiel Command
chunks.text:
Provide TNGDEV the first production or procurement item or system and peculiar support equip-
ment to enable institutional training by the FUE date.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Commanding General, U.S. Army Materiel Command
chunks.text:
Provide NET and technical documentation and supporting training materials to units conducting
AWEs and advanced technology demonstrations, in conjunction with TNGDEV, CAPDEV, or procuring
agency. Coordinate AMT policy issues with DCS, G-3/5/7 (Training Directorate), Washington, DC 20310-
0450.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Commanding General, U.S. Army Materiel Command
chunks.text: Support Civilian leader development (training and education) within the command.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Commanding General, U.S. Army Materiel Command
chunks.text:
Review and provide input for updates to ARTEP Products and Standard METL. Participate in the
STRAG METL approval process.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Commanding General, U.S. Army Materiel Command
chunks.text:
Develop the necessary controls to ensure subordinate command, U.S. Army Sustainment Command,
comply with guidance in the ITDLM Handbook.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Commanding General, U.S. Army Materiel Command
chunks.text:
Oversee subsistence payment process for Soldiers directed meals while attending Institutional Train-
ing under the ITDLM Program.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-23. Commanding General, U.S. Army Futures Command
chunks.text:
Act as the designated advisor of the Equipping PEG to lead efforts to define and assess the Future
Operational Environment; develop and deliver operational and (warfighting) functional concepts; develop
and deliver future force designs; and support the delivery of materiel solutions in accordance with the
Army Modernization priorities generally focused on the force 5-15 years in the future.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-23. Commanding General, U.S. Army Futures Command
chunks.text:
Determine required capabilities needed to fulfill Army and Joint requirements and, when delegated
by the Chief of Staff of the Army, approve requirements for equipping the Army.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-23. Commanding General, U.S. Army Futures Command
chunks.text:
Assist the ASA (ALT) with developing policies, processes, and procedures to support materiel inte-
gration to include TADSS within AFC’s business processes.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-23. Commanding General, U.S. Army Futures Command
chunks.text:
Coordinate with TRADOC (CAC and Force Modernization Proponents) to ensure the synchroniza-
tion of DOTmLPF-P, in particular the identification and documentation in Army Modernization capability
requirements documents, of training support requirements for all training domains.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-24. Commanding General, U.S. Army Corps of Engineers
chunks.text:
Provide technical advice and assistance to the Army Staff pertaining to facilities, military engineer-
ing, real estate, and geospatial individual and unit training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-24. Commanding General, U.S. Army Corps of Engineers
chunks.text:
Coordinate with the TNGDEV, MATDEV, and ACOMs to identify MILCON & SRM requirements to
support AMT, and ensure the Facilities Support Plan supports scheduled fielding of materiel systems.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-24. Commanding General, U.S. Army Corps of Engineers
chunks.text:
Develop and conduct training for civil works programs and for military programs assigned as USACE
support functions.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-24. Commanding General, U.S. Army Corps of Engineers
chunks.text:
Develop the necessary controls to ensure subordinate activity (Norfolk District) comply with guid-
ance in the ITDLM Handbook.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-24. Commanding General, U.S. Army Corps of Engineers
chunks.text:
Represent the interests of DCS, G-3/5/7, ARNG and USARC in providing Privatization of Army
Lodging (PAL) for Soldiers attending Institutional Training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-24. Commanding General, U.S. Army Corps of Engineers
chunks.text: Execute the PAL Lodging In-License Agreements to support of the ITDLM Program.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-24. Commanding General, U.S. Army Corps of Engineers
chunks.text: Oversee lodging validation process in support of the ITDLM Program.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-24. Commanding General, U.S. Army Corps of Engineers
chunks.text: Oversee lodging payment process with the PAL vendor.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-25. Commanding General, U.S. Army Test and Evaluation Command
chunks.text:
The CG, ATEC will assist with TSS training enabler planning and implement the sustainable range pro-
gram at ATEC test centers.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-26. Commandant, U.S. Army War College
chunks.text:
Educate and develop leaders for service at the strategic level while advancing knowledge in the
global application of landpower.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-26. Commandant, U.S. Army War College
chunks.text:
As the lead agent for the Army’s General Officer education, execute oversight on behalf of the CSA
and CG, TRADOC.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-26. Commandant, U.S. Army War College
chunks.text:
Oversee the 10-month resident course of instruction qualifying attendees for award of MEL-1, a
Masters of Strategic Studies degree, and Joint professional military education (JPME II), subject to the
approval of the Secretary of the Army and consistent with HQDA and CJCS policy.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-26. Commandant, U.S. Army War College
chunks.text:
Oversee a 2-year distance education course of instruction that qualifies attendees for award of
MEL-1, a Masters of Strategic Studies degree, and JPME I/II, subject to the approval of the SA and con-
sistent with HQDA and CJCS policy.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-26. Commandant, U.S. Army War College
chunks.text:
Oversee the USAWC Fellows Program (MEL1), CSA, and SMA Senior Fellow Programs in accord-
ance with AR 621-7, G-3/5/7 policy, and CSA Guidance.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-26. Commandant, U.S. Army War College
chunks.text: Conduct an International Fellowship Program in support of all educational courses at USAWC.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-26. Commandant, U.S. Army War College
chunks.text:
Oversee a course of instruction that provides Army, Joint and select partners the qualifications and
unique skills, knowledge, and attributes to excel as strategists subject to coordination with the FA59 pro-
ponent and consistent with HQDA policy.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-26. Commandant, U.S. Army War College
chunks.text:
Oversee courses, programs, and instruction to educate and prepare senior leaders to use data and
Artificial Intelligence to enable warfighting decisions.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-26. Commandant, U.S. Army War College
chunks.text:
Oversee a course of instruction that provides ASCC Staffs the qualifications and unique skills,
knowledge, and attributes to excel as Theater Army Staffs.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-26. Commandant, U.S. Army War College
chunks.text:
Execute the Army’s responsibilities to provide courses and instruction on Combined and Joint Land
Component Commands.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-26. Commandant, U.S. Army War College
chunks.text:
Oversee the Center for Strategic Leadership (CSL) as the Army’s strategic wargaming center and a
thought and education leader for strategic landpower and futures.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-26. Commandant, U.S. Army War College
chunks.text:
Oversee of the Strategic Studies Institute (SSI) and Army War College Press as the Army’s premier
research agency for examining strategic security challenges.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-26. Commandant, U.S. Army War College
chunks.text:
Oversee the U.S. Army Heritage and Education Center (USAHEC) to provide archives, library sup-
port and research assistance to USAWC students and faculty.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-26. Commandant, U.S. Army War College
chunks.text:
Coordinate and attain accreditation with external organizations for degree/MOS/ASI/PDSI/and Joint
Military Professional Education credit producing programs for the external accreditation/review of
MOS/ASI-producing courses provide by faculty for USAWC.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-26. Commandant, U.S. Army War College
chunks.text:
Oversee the Nominative Leader Course to provide a strategic education in preparation for CSMs to
serve at the strategic command and organizational level.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-27. Other commanders and leaders
chunks.text:
Senior commanders, division commanders, state adjutants general, and USAR major commands
will exercise command relationship responsibilities with regard to: assigned missions (as applicable); de-
veloping training guidance and master training plans; allocating the necessary resources to effectively im-
plement these plans; evaluating the conduct of training; reducing training detractors; reducing personnel
and unit turbulence; and providing subordinate commanders maximum time to train (see AR 600-20). To
improve training, commanders will employ the ATMS (DTMS, Army Training Network, CATS) or next gen-
eration capability, to plan, resource, and manage unit training and access Army standard training
products. ATMS is applicable to Garrison TDA organizations where feasible or as required by the DCS,
G-3/5/7. Practice the doctrinal principles (seeper ADP/FM 7-0, and ADP/FM 6-22.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-27. Other commanders and leaders
chunks.text:
Identify and reduce training detractors. Ensure that Army-approved training products such as the
ARTEP, Standard METL, CATS, TSPs, training and evaluation outlines (T&EOs), and Soldier manuals
are understood and used. Commanders are responsible for the content of any training materiel not pro-
vided by HQDA, or ACOM/ASCC/DRU.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-27. Other commanders and leaders
chunks.text:
Ensure that Army-approved management tools are used, such as ATMS (Army Training Network,
DTMS, CATS) and Training Ammunition Management Information System.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-27. Other commanders and leaders
chunks.text:
Conduct HQDA mandatory training requirements within their commands. Minimize on-line delivery
by emphasizing face-to-face delivery.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-27. Other commanders and leaders
chunks.text:
Review and provide input for updates to ARTEP Products and Standard METL. Participate in the
STRAG METL approval process.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-27. Other commanders and leaders
chunks.text: Encourage and support Soldiers’ participation in self-improvement and Civilian education courses.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-27. Other commanders and leaders
chunks.text:
Leveraging military training for support of civil authorities. Any 10 USC Army unit training off-base
will do so in accordance with DoDI 1322.28 and DoDI 3025.21. Actions include: coordination with law en-
forcement agencies (LEA) when appropriate to provide an orientation briefing to the military personnel
involved in the off-base training, as well as plans for a medical evacuation plan of military personnel in-
volved in the event; a communication plan for military elements involved in the event; and to meet other
administrative and operational requirements with input from LEA and/or local military police/security force
personnel. When applicable, use state installation regulations and SOPs to identify local coordination and
safety requirements for ARNG training. Unless otherwise stated by DCS, G-3/5/7, all units will comply
with event risk assessment criteria, approval authority levels and notification levels outlined in DoDI
1322.28.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-28. Responsibilities for programs of instruction/courses at special schools
chunks.text:
Pursuant to 5160.48, Defense Information School POIs/courses rests with the Office of the Assistant
Secretary of Defense (Public Affairs).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-28. Responsibilities for programs of instruction/courses at special schools
chunks.text:
The CG, The Judge Advocate General’s Legal Center and School approves POIs/courses for legal
education courses.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-28. Responsibilities for programs of instruction/courses at special schools
chunks.text:
The Commandant, USAWC, develops USAWC curriculum and forwards it to CG, TRADOC for re-
view. The Commandant, USAWC, and the CG, TRADOC, coordinate the curriculum to ensure that the
education program is consistent with Army University and OES objectives.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-28. Responsibilities for programs of instruction/courses at special schools
chunks.text:
The Director, U.S. Army Criminal Investigation Division, supervises the Army crime laboratory sys-
tem in accordance with AR 195-2 and operates the Criminal Investigation Laboratory.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-28. Responsibilities for programs of instruction/courses at special schools
chunks.text:
Pursuant to CJCSI 1801.01G, The President, National Defense University, approves the curriculum
for National Defense University colleges and institutions.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-28. Responsibilities for programs of instruction/courses at special schools
chunks.text: The Commandant, DLIFLC-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-28. Responsibilities for programs of instruction/courses at special schools
chunks.text: Approves POIs/courses for foreign language training.
