leader guidance. Commanders approve the selected tasks, provide the resources, allocate time to pre-
pare, train and certify NCOs leading training, and monitor the training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 5 > 5-12. Airborne training in units
chunks.text: Individual proficiency training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 5 > 5-12. Airborne training in units
chunks.text:
All Soldiers filling paid parachute positions must perform, at a minimum, one jump per quarter to
maintain currency and qualification for hazardous duty pay. In addition, these Soldiers will conduct re-
fresher training once a month.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 5 > 5-12. Airborne training in units
chunks.text:
Soldiers assigned to an airborne unit filling a paid parachute position are prioritized for jumps as
follows:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 5 > 5-12. Airborne training in units
chunks.text:
Priority 1: Soldiers assigned to USASOC elements aligned with a crisis response plan, 75th Ranger
Regiment, and the Department of the Army ordered IRF Brigade Task force will complete 12 jumps per
year.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 5 > 5-12. Airborne training in units
chunks.text:
Priority 2: Soldiers assigned to OCONUS Airborne IBCTs will be resourced to battalion level profi-
ciency with brigade C2.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 5 > 5-12. Airborne training in units
chunks.text:
Priority 3: Component 2 and 3 Soldiers filling paid parachute positions will conduct 4 jumps per year
to maintain currency. (Total number of jumps includes the DOD mandated four jumps per year specified
in para 5-12a(1) to meet pay requirements.)

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 5 > 5-12. Airborne training in units
chunks.text:
Priority 1 and Priority 2 units will ensure 50 percent of jumps are night jumps and 75 percent must
be from high performance aircraft.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 5 > 5-12. Airborne training in units
chunks.text:
Commanders will determine exact jump frequency for their units based on mission and available
resources. Training requirements will remain at the discretion of unit commanders.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 5 > 5-12. Airborne training in units
chunks.text: Collective training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 5 > 5-12. Airborne training in units
chunks.text:
Airborne IBCT assault command posts and its assault battalions, with the mission to provide forces
to the Army component of the IRF/CRF, will conduct at least one-night airborne mass tactical assault per
quarter. This training should include at least 12 heavy equipment platforms and 16 containerized delivery
systems, followed by a field training exercise which includes the seizure of an assault objective. When
plausible, training should also include the air-landed portion of follow-on forces and enablers.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 5 > 5-12. Airborne training in units
chunks.text:
Units identified as critical enablers to the airborne IBCT IRF/CRF mission will execute at least 8
combat equipment jumps from a high-performance aircraft per year. Critical enabling units attached to an
airborne IBCT preparing or assuming the IRF/CRF mission, will execute one combat equipment jump per
month and participate in the airborne IBCT collective training outlined in paragraph 5-12b(1) to include
heavy equipment platforms appropriate to support training objectives.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 5 > 5-12. Airborne training in units
chunks.text:
Corps and division assault command posts identified as mission command headquarters for air-
borne IBCTs with the mission to provide forces to the Army component of the CRF will execute, at a mini-
mum, one night airborne mass tactical assault per quarter in conjunction with IBCT airborne collective
training in paragraph 5-12b(1).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 5 > 5-12. Airborne training in units
chunks.text:
The Joint Forcible Entry Exercises allow outstanding opportunities for Joint training. The main goal
of the Joint Forcible Entry Exercises is to enhance Army and Air Force interoperability and rehearse/vali-
date Joint Forcible Entry Operations. The division with the mission of providing airborne IBCTs to the
Army IRF/CRF component should execute 6 to 8 Joint Forcible Entry Exercises annually. The priority will
go to IBCTs preparing to assume the IRF/CRF mission or sustain IRF/CRF forcible entry proficiency.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 5 > 5-12. Airborne training in units
chunks.text:
Joint Airborne/Air Transportability Training. JA/ATT details, procedures and CONUS JA/ATT priori-
ties can be found in appendix E.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 5 > 5-13. Troop schools training
chunks.text:
Troop schools help to support training execution and training management at the command or Army
installation level. Commanders will use troop schools as part of their unit training strategy to acquire, en-
hance, sustain, and supplement individual military skills or pre-command education not readily available
through the institutional training base. These military skills should be acquired by the Soldier early enough
in the unit’s training cycle in order to optimize this Soldier’s contribution to the unit. These military skills
include but are not limited to air assault operations, arms room security, mine resistant ambush protected
vehicle tactical driving, air load planning, Company Commander/First Sergeant Course, and DTMS. There
are four types of courses offered at troop schools: (1) Proponent Courses, (2) Operational Courses, (3)
Unit Courses and (4) Garrison Courses.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 5 > 5-13. Troop schools training
chunks.text:
Troop schools are under the command and control of the respective ACOM, ASCC, or DRU (less
IMCOM) at the installation level or a training center. Course programs of instruction and associated train-
ing support materials used at troop schools are required to be coordinated and approved with the training
proponent responsible for the “taught skill” regardless of instructor type (for example, military, Army Civil-
ian, or contractor). A comprehensive list of all training proponents is maintained by Army University. Pro-
ponent courses are developed by the proponent and delivered in support of the proponent training plan.
Proponent courses are subject to AQAP accreditation reviews. Operational courses are developed by the
proponent but are delivered in support of the unit training plan. Instructors for both courses must meet
proponent certification requirements for the courses.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 5 > 5-13. Troop schools training
chunks.text:
The garrison command does not command or control a troop school. They will not provide military
skill instruction to Soldiers. However, garrison command can provide instruction through a garrison in-
structional program for non-military or Family support related skills, education, or information. This instruc-
tional program would include instruction such as motorcycle safety, equipment use instruction (use of
snow blowers and lawn mowers), non-tactical vehicle drivers training, family readiness, morale and recre-
ation services (swimming and hunter safety), prevention of alcohol and drug abuse, and family financial
management. Garrison courses will comply with training proponent requirements or local laws and poli-
cies as applicable. Garrison course instructors will meet proponent certification standards, or those stand-
ards set forth by local law or policy.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 5 > 5-13. Troop schools training
chunks.text:
Unit courses are developed by the troop school command and conducted in support of the unit train-
ing plan when there is no existing proponent course to support the training need.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 5 > 5-13. Troop schools training
chunks.text:
Troop school courses of instruction do not relieve officers, WOs, or NCOs from their roles as train-
ers. Accordingly, troop schools will not conduct courses of instruction in-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 5 > 5-13. Troop schools training
chunks.text:
Tactical or combat-related training that the chain of command has inherent responsibility to train
(for example rifle marksmanship, physical readiness training, or rappelling).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 5 > 5-13. Troop schools training
chunks.text: Training in warrior tasks and battle drills.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 5 > 5-13. Troop schools training
chunks.text: Training on collective tasks (for example: training for crews, teams, squads, and platoons).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 5 > 5-13. Troop schools training
chunks.text:
Commands will promulgate regulatory guidance for the operation, management, and funding of
Troop Schools. Guidance will include:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 5 > 5-13. Troop schools training
chunks.text: Approval of all contractors provided courses of instruction by the applicable ACOM.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 5 > 5-13. Troop schools training
chunks.text:
Identification of infrastructure, sustainment and TSS enterprise resources required to execute troop
school requirements.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 5 > 5-13. Troop schools training
chunks.text:
Programs of instruction and associated training support materials must be approved by the applica-
ble proponent school regardless of instructor support (for example, military, Army Civilian, or contractor).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 5 > 5-13. Troop schools training
chunks.text:
Troop Schools, if required, can provide administrative, classroom and manpower support to the DL
courses.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 5 > 5-13. Troop schools training
chunks.text:
Courses subject to the development and approval of a training proponent (proponent and opera-
tional courses, and applicable garrison courses) will be scheduled through ATRRS. ATRRS Course com-
pletions will be documented in ATRRS and in DTMS in the Soldier’s ITR.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 5 > 5-13. Troop schools training
chunks.text: NET and language training are not governed under troop schools.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6
chunks.text: Training Support System

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-1. Purpose
chunks.text:
The Training Support System enables the Army’s three training domains-operational, institutional and
self-development. TSS delivers relevant live, virtual/gaming, and constructive (LVGC) training enablers
through the Army’s TSS products, services, and facilities. It creates/training conditions that realistically
portray the Unified Action Training Environment and enables METL-based operational training strategies
within the Sustainable Readiness construct, as well as institutional strategies reflected in applicable POI
for IET, PME, and functional training. TSS also provides core infrastructure that enables the self-develop-
ment domain see AR 350-52.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-2. Training support system programs
chunks.text:
The Army’s TSS provides management of training support functions and programs, and maintains fielded
non-system, and approved system TADSS, world-wide, supporting Army training goals. TSS programs
are managed by a series of venues. The TSS includes three major lines of effort that provide holistic de-
velopment and delivery of training products, services, and facilities to TSS executors. The TSS lines of
effort are:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-2. Training support system programs
chunks.text:
Live Training includes the SRP, the CTC Modernization Program, and the live enablers from the
TSS portfolio. The Live Training LoE establishes policy, procedures, and responsibilities for Army wide
management of capabilities codified in AR 350-52, AR 350-50, AR 385-63, TC 25-1, TC 25-8, and AR
420-1 supporting range and training area management (infrastructure and maintenance).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-2. Training support system programs
chunks.text:
Synthetic Training: Comprised of the Mission Command Training Support Program (MCTSP) and
those synthetic environment portions of the Soldier Training Support Program (see AR 350-52 and AR
350-38).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-2. Training support system programs
chunks.text: Training Support Integration: TSS Integration LoE is comprised of (see AR 350-52 and AR 350-38):

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-2. Training support system programs
chunks.text: TSS management services and overhead.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-2. Training support system programs
chunks.text:
The Training Information Infrastructure (TII) Program consists of ATIS (products) and point of deliv-
ery (PoD) (services) capabilities.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-2. Training support system programs
chunks.text:
The Army TADSS Maintenance Program provides resources for the operations and maintenance of
nonsystem and TGOSC approved system TADSS.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7
chunks.text:
Army Modernization Training
Section I
Introduction

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-1. Policies and procedures
chunks.text:
This chapter prescribes policy for providing training and training support with the fielding or issue of
new/improved/displaced equipment. Unless specifically stated elsewhere, the information in this chapter
applies to all components of the Army.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-1. Policies and procedures
chunks.text:
Critical collective and individual tasks requiring training and training support requirements that must
be developed and fielded with a materiel system are identified in accordance with the Manual for the Op-
eration of the JCIDS (hereafter referred to as the JCIDS Manual). A system training KPP is intended to
ensure that the materiel system is fielded with this critical training capability. Properly describing a system
training KPP also ensures that life cycle costs of training are considered for the program.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-1. Policies and procedures
chunks.text:
System training is an essential aspect of AMT and is found in the JCIDS Manual. The training is in-
tended to ensure that materiel aspects of training capabilities, when applicable, are addressed as part of
the development of the capability solution outlined in the capability development document. (The Training
is applicable to all capability development documents with materiel training requirements which dictate
specific operational performance characteristics of the capability solution). In order to integrate system
training capabilities, training must be considered early in the capabilities development and DOTMLPF-P
assessment process. AR 71-9 reflects the following requirement documents: ICD, capability development
document (CDD), CDD-update (CDD-U), Abbreviated Capability Development Document (A-CDD) (for
prototype systems), and capability drop (CD) (for software-based systems). To comply with the JCIDS
Manual, sponsors of Army capabilities documents (Army CAPDEV, TNGDEV and system proponents)
must:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-1. Policies and procedures
chunks.text: Determine if system training is a key performance parameter.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-1. Policies and procedures
chunks.text:
If the analysis determines that system training should not be a KPP, a summary of the justification
will be provided in the CDD (usually in paragraph 5 of the requirements document entitled “System Capa-
bilities required for the Current Increment”).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-1. Policies and procedures
chunks.text: If analysis determines that system training should be a KPP, then sponsors must:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-1. Policies and procedures
chunks.text:
Ensure system training and training support is addressed in analysis of alternatives and subse-
quent phases;

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-1. Policies and procedures
chunks.text:
Ensure projected training requirements and associated costs are appropriately addressed across
the program life cycle; and

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-1. Policies and procedures
chunks.text:
Include results of sustainment training analysis in their capabilities document submission along with
an overall recommendation to the Joint Capability Board and/or Joint Requirements Oversight Council on
sustainment training applicability.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-1. Policies and procedures
chunks.text:
The total training concept, strategy, and training support system resourcing estimates for integrating
the system or family of systems into the operational, institutional, and self-development domains are pro-
vided in an annex to the approved JCIDS capabilities document called the STRAP. Non-materiel aspects
of training and training support are to be captured as part of the DOTMLPF-P considerations and pro-
gram affordability paragraphs of the capability development document. The system cost-benefit analysis
is a requirement for the CDD in accordance with AR 71-9. The STRAP contains the comprehensive back-
ground, justification, and details for all training and training support requirements. The STRAP will be an
extension of the training information contained in the capability requirements document and will provide
additional training support details that align with proponent Training Strategy. It is not a mandatory docu-
ment for the capability development document, but should be submitted to support the training paragraph
outlined in the CDD.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-1. Policies and procedures
chunks.text:
The STRAP is the master training plan and training tool for a new or modified system. It is prepared
to support a TSS that meets the training requirements of the warfighter. It outlines the development of the
total training concept, strategy, and TSS resourcing estimates for integrating the system or family of sys-
tems into the operational, institutional, and self-development domains. The training estimate costs of the
STRAP will be embedded in the CDD’s Program Affordability Table. The STRAP will delineate:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-1. Policies and procedures
chunks.text:
Required and critical collective and individual task training and training support needed in training
base schools.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-1. Policies and procedures
chunks.text:
Required and critical collective and individual task training and training support needed to train op-
erators and maintainers when equipment is fielded.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-1. Policies and procedures
chunks.text:
Required and critical collective and individual task training and training support needed so that unit
leaders can achieve and sustain unit proficiency after fielding.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-1. Policies and procedures
chunks.text:
Required and critical collective and individual task training and training support needed for web-
based self-development and knowledge management tools.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-1. Policies and procedures
chunks.text:
Required and critical collective and individual task capabilities for the existing TSS for schools,
units, Army training centers, mobilization sites, and deployable training capability.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-1. Policies and procedures
chunks.text:
Required and critical collective and individual task training and training support needed in training
support units.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-1. Policies and procedures
chunks.text:
Required collective and individual task integration into existing and planned system and non-sys-
tem training aids, devices, simulators and simulations.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-1. Policies and procedures
chunks.text:
Force modernization proponents staff draft STRAPs for peer and training domain staffing to the
mandatory external agencies designated in the SWT. All STRAP development efforts have TSS and sus-
tainment training equities that inform and may impact the three training domains. STRAPs and STRAP
waiver must be CAC-T validated and COE, CG (or commandant) approved prior to submission to the
AFC Pre-CAMS Entry Gate for Army Staffing. TRADOC approval is prior to CDD staffing packet submis-
sion to AFC for proponent worldwide staffing release.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-1. Policies and procedures
chunks.text:
Proponents develop an initial-STRAP (I-STRAP) concurrently with the development of an A-CDD.
There is no waiver process for an I-STRAP. The intended purpose of an I-STRAP is to identify system
training capabilities required across training domains (to include combat training centers’ costing for the
opposing forces (OPFOR)), to resource rough order of magnitude (ROM) costs based on assumptions the
system will transition to a materiel program in the future. I-STRAPs (concurrent with A-CDDs) become the
basis for supporting and informing materiel program STRAP development concurrent with CDD develop-
ment.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-1. Policies and procedures
chunks.text:
TP 350-70-13 provides TNGDEVs with details and referenced tools for STRAP, I-STRAP, and
STRAP waiver development to support efforts:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-1. Policies and procedures
chunks.text:
The STRAP Writing Tool (SWT) is the Army's system-of-record for developing, staffing, and ap-
proval of STRAPs and I-STRAPS.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-1. Policies and procedures
chunks.text:
STRAPs and STRAP waivers are developed by the CoE/TNGDEVs with the CAPDEVs and
MATDEVs, in support of Army-approved materiel programs, validated by CAC-T/TSAID, and approved by
CoE Branch Commander or Commandant.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-1. Policies and procedures
chunks.text:
I-STRAP are developed by the CoE/TNGDEVs with the CAPDEVs and MATDEVs in support of
prototype system requirement document development, conveys capability assumptions and rough-order-
of-magnitude (ROM) of resources of a prototyped system toward a material capability determination are
concurred by CAC-T/TSAID. The CoE branch commander or commandant may delegate approval author-
ity down to the CoE/DoT O-6/GS-15.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-1. Policies and procedures
chunks.text:
The Central Army Registry (CAR) is the Army official repository for approved STRAPs and STRAP
waivers.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-1. Policies and procedures
chunks.text:
STRAP waivers are staffed to HQ TRADOC, CAC-T, FORSCOM and ACOMs for determination of
training requirements, equities, and concurrence prior to CAC-T validation and CoE branch commander
or commandant approval.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-1. Policies and procedures
chunks.text:
Generally, collective and individual task training and training support requirements for a materiel
system are identified by the TNGDEV and are validated and prioritized, as required, by DCS, G-3/5/7
(Training Directorate).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-2. Provision of training and training support
chunks.text:
Funding responsibilities. The program manager (PM) is the total life cycle system manager, respon-
sible for total life cycle management of all training capability embedded in a materiel system. Additionally,
the PM is responsible for fielding a fully supportable system (to include the Integrated Logistic Service el-
ement - training and training support) and integrating the materiel system’s training subsystem into the
Army TSS. Accordingly, the PM funds operator/crew/maintainer training required to field a new/im-
prove/displaced system as well as operator/crew/maintainer training support for fielding, for sustainment
training after fielding, and for training base schools. Tables 7-1 and 7-2 clarify responsibilities for identify-
ing, approving, funding, and/or producing various types of training support.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-2. Provision of training and training support
chunks.text:
The PM should ensure that the TRADOC proponent is provided initial sets, to include prototypes of
equipment, at least 1 year prior (goal) to FUE or as directed by respective HQDA policy, to facilitate the
proponent working in parallel with the MATDEVs TNGDEV to validate training products and prepare the
necessary training products for training throughout the Army. Per AR 70-1, the acquisition of a training
system will have the same priority as the support (parent) system or equipment. To ensure that the train-
ing base has the capability to train operators, maintainers, and leaders on the materiel system within one
year of FUE date (goal) or as directed by respective HQDA policy, it will be necessary for the MATDEV to
program for the fielding of systems or items of equipment, to the training base to enable the introduction
of the new system into the LVC environments and all training domains.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-2. Provision of training and training support
chunks.text:
Categories of training. The following categories of training are used to define responsibilities for the
provision of training and training support:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-2. Provision of training and training support
chunks.text:
New equipment training. The initial transfer of knowledge on the operation and maintenance of
new and improved equipment from the MATDEV to the tester, trainer, supporter, and user.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-2. Provision of training and training support
chunks.text:
Displaced equipment training. Training provided by the PM on the operation and maintenance of
previously fielded equipment that is scheduled for redistribution within an ACOM, ASCC, or DRU or
among several ACOMs, ASCCs, or DRUs or components because of the Army Modernization Process.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-2. Provision of training and training support
chunks.text:
Doctrine and tactics training. Training provided by the TNGDEV on employment, tactics, and in-
teroperability of new or displaced equipment.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-2. Provision of training and training support
chunks.text:
Sustainment training. Individual and collective task training conducted by and within a unit, or or-
ganization, to ensure continued expertise on the operation, maintenance, and employment of fielded
equipment upon the completion of NET/DET and after Soldiers who PCS, ETS, or retire from units are
replaced.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-2. Provision of training and training support
chunks.text:
Key players. The following agencies have distinct responsibilities for providing training and training
support:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-2. Provision of training and training support
chunks.text:
Program and/or product manager. In accordance with DoDD 5000.01, “The Defense Acquisition
System “and additional applicable directives, instructions and regulations, “...the PM is accountable for
achieving program life cycle management objectives throughout the program life cycle. Planning for oper-
ations and support will begin at program inception, and supportability requirements will be balanced with
other requirements that impact program cost, schedule, and performance. Performance based life cycle
product support implements life cycle system management.” They exercise leadership, decision-making,
and oversight throughout a program and a systems life cycle. They need to be the leader of the program,
understand requirements, balance constraints, manage contractors, build support, and use the basic
skills of management.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-2. Provision of training and training support
chunks.text:
Authority. The MDA is the designated individual with overall responsibility for a program. The MDA
will have the authority to approve entry of an acquisition program into the next phase of the acquisition
process and will be accountable for cost, schedule, and performance reporting to higher authority, includ-
ing Congressional reporting. The Under Secretary of Defense (USD) for Acquisition and Sustainment
(A&S) is the MDA for all major Acquisition Category (ACAT) 1 programs unless delegated.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-2. Provision of training and training support
chunks.text:
Materiel developer. The MATDEV is the research, development, and acquisition (RDA) command,
agency, or office assigned responsibility for the system under development, or being acquired. The term
may be used generically to refer to the RDA community in the materiel acquisition process (for example,
PEO, PM, Class I, II, and III level managers).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-2. Provision of training and training support
chunks.text:
Capability developer. The CAPDEV is the command or agency that formulates warfighting require-
ments for DOTLMPF-P. The acronym CAPDEV may be used generically to represent the user and user
maintainer community role in the materiel acquisition process (counterpart to generic use of MATDEV).
ACMs within AFC and USASMDC and TPOs within TRADOC are the managers of selected capability ar-
eas and ACAT I, ACAT II, or other high priority materiel systems when a need exists for management out-
side the normal capacity available to force modernization proponents for capability development integra-
tion, synchronization, and accomplishing user requirements in the materiel acquisition process. ACMs
and TPOs working for and reporting to the force modernization proponent, are chartered by AFC or
TRADOC respectively to manage and integrate DOTMLPF-P for the assigned capability area(s). Within
the training area, the ACMs or TPO advise and should assist in the development of home station and in-
stitutional training for individuals, crews and units-including the development and fielding of training aids,
devices (system and non-system), simulations and simulators for use in training in the institution, home
station, and CTCs.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-2. Provision of training and training support
chunks.text:
Training developer. TNGDEV is the Army agency or individual that determines requirements for a
system’s training subsystem and formulates, develops, and documents associated training concepts, pro-
ponent training strategies, plans, and required training support. The TNGDEV is a subset of and included
within the TRADOC training Proponent Center or School and serves as the user’s representative during
development and acquisition of a system’s training subsystem. TNGDEVs must be included far left in the
capability development/DOTMLPF-P assessment process with other Force Modernization Proponents
and be responsive to all Adaptive Acquisition Pathways in accordance with AR 70-1, to include, Major Ca-
pability Acquisition/JCIDS process, Urgent Capability Acquisition (UCA), Middle Tier of Acquisition (MTA),
and Software. The STRAP Writing Tool (SWT) is the Army’s system of record for developing, staffing,
and approving STRAPs and I-STRAPs. STRAPs and STRAP waivers are approved by CoE Branch Com-
mander or Commandant. CAC-T validates STRAPs and STRAP waivers prior to approval. I-STRAPS
can be approved by the CoE Director of Training (DOT), CoE CG, or Commandant. CAC-T concurs with
all I-STRAPS prior to approval.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-2. Provision of training and training support
chunks.text:
New Equipment Training Manager. Official designated by the PM responsible for planning, coordi-
nating, and conducting NET.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-3. Training developer determines requirements for new equipment training and doctrine and
chunks.text:
tactics training
When new/improved or displaced equipment is issued to a unit, the TRADOC Proponent TNGDEV will
assess what training development and training support needs to accompany equipment fielding. In gen-
eral, the PM is responsible for providing any needed operator/crew/maintainer training and opera-
tor/crew/maintainer training support via a NET and the TRADOC TNGDEV is responsible for providing
any unit training and unit training support required to support the systems integration into the Army. A
doctrine, training and tactics (DTT) team from the proponent may be required to incorporate the system
into a unit, which is being restructured, and so on. Requirements for NET and DTT are established in the
program capability document and/or the STRAP.
Section II
New Equipment Training

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-4. New equipment training objective
chunks.text:
NET provides the initial transfer of knowledge on the operation and maintenance of new/improved and
displaced equipment from MATDEV to the tester, trainer, supporter, and user. NET will assist command-
ers to achieve operational capability in the shortest time practical by training Soldiers/crews how to oper-
ate and maintain the new/improved equipment and by providing unit leaders with training support compo-
nents needed to sustain proficiency of operators and maintainers on the new/improved equipment after
NET. This may include actual live fire for individual or crew qualification.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-5. New equipment training policy
chunks.text: Begin planning for NET at the onset of program initiation.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-5. New equipment training policy
chunks.text:
Employ the most cost-effective and feasible training strategy to conduct NET which conforms to the
STRAP.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-5. New equipment training policy
chunks.text:
Include in NET, training on the training support components provided at NET that enable unit lead-
ers to sustain operator and maintainer proficiency on the new/improved equipment after NET.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-5. New equipment training policy
chunks.text:
Coordinate plans for NET training and training support with MATDEV, TNGDEV, CAPDEV, testers,
and user ACOMs, ASCCs, and DRUs.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-5. New equipment training policy
chunks.text: Coordinate NET funding requirements with designated PEOs and PMs.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-5. New equipment training policy
chunks.text:
Ensure NET support components are developed in accordance with the Army Training and Educa-
tion Development (ED) process and Army training support design standards.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-5. New equipment training policy
chunks.text:
Ensure that MATDEV developed NET training material and products comply with training and edu-
cation standards and requirements in accordance with AR 350-1 and TRADOC regulations and support-
ing pamphlets.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-5. New equipment training policy
chunks.text:
Input all NET training material and products into the Training Development Capability (TDC), vali-
dated in accordance with this regulation and TRADOC 350-70 and supporting TRADOC pamphlets.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-5. New equipment training policy
chunks.text:
Ensure embedded training requirements and computer-based instructions are developed in accord-
ance with the system capabilities document during the initial development process.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-5. New equipment training policy
chunks.text:
Ensure effectiveness of NET and training support components are validated (by the responsible
training proponent(s)) and on-hand before the conduct of NET.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-5. New equipment training policy
chunks.text:
Review basis of issue plan feeder data for new and improved equipment in accordance with AR 71-
32.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-5. New equipment training policy
chunks.text:
Prepare a Qualitative and Quantitative Personnel Requirements Information document in accordance
with AR 71-32.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-5. New equipment training policy
chunks.text:
Prepare a NET plan for each system requiring NET within 30 days of forwarding a Qualitative and
Quantitative Personnel Requirements Information Data Interchange Package to the Army TNGDEV
through the U.S. Army Force Management Support Activity.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-5. New equipment training policy
chunks.text:
Conform with Federal Acquisition Regulation when procuring NET services from contractor person-
nel.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-5. New equipment training policy
chunks.text:
Provide NET support for other military departments for multi-Service systems or equipment when
the Army is the lead service.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-5. New equipment training policy
chunks.text:
When NET awards an ASI, record in ATRRS the participation of personnel in NET, including reser-
vations for, enrollments in, and graduation from NET.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-6. Planning for new equipment training
chunks.text: Review the requirements document to identify NET training requirements.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-6. Planning for new equipment training
chunks.text: Work with TNGDEV to develop a NET training strategy.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-6. Planning for new equipment training
chunks.text:
Work with TNGDEV to develop NET activities/events for operators/crews/maintainers and associ-
ated training support.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-6. Planning for new equipment training
chunks.text:
Work with TNGDEV to develop training support needed by field units and training base schools to
sustain proficiency of operators/crews/maintainers after NET.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-6. Planning for new equipment training
chunks.text:
Consolidate manpower and funding requirements for NET on the new/improved system and for de-
veloping/ fielding the training support needed by field units and training base schools to sustain profi-
ciency of operators/crews/maintainers after NET.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-6. Planning for new equipment training
chunks.text:
Review Basis of Issue Plan Feeder Data for program milestones, system description, system de-
ployment, recommended quantity, and submit recommended changes to the PEOs and PMs, as required.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-6. Planning for new equipment training
chunks.text:
Prepare the Qualitative and Quantitative Personnel Requirements Information in accordance with
AR 71-32.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-6. Planning for new equipment training
chunks.text:
Forward the completed Qualitative & Quantitative Personnel Requirements Information to the Army
TNGDEV through the U.S. Army Force Management Support Activity according to the time frames and
guidelines established in AR 71-32.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-6. Planning for new equipment training
chunks.text:
Ensure the Qualitative and Quantitative Personnel Requirements Information is forwarded to U.S.
Army Force Management Support Activity through the Army Materiel Plan Modernization system.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-6. Planning for new equipment training
chunks.text:
Plan for the provision of the training and training support needed to execute NET strategy, for ex-
ample, the provision of how-to-fight doctrine, training support publications (Soldier’s Manuals and CATS),
training courses, training support packages, NET team members, facilities, and so forth.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-6. Planning for new equipment training
chunks.text: Estimate travel, per diem, and TDY costs in support of NET.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-6. Planning for new equipment training
chunks.text:
Estimate contractor expenses, prepare independent Government cost estimates, and budget re-
quirement packages.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-7. New equipment training planning considerations
chunks.text: When planning for NET, the following factors must be taken into consideration by the NET manager:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-7. New equipment training planning considerations
chunks.text: The technical complexity of the equipment and its similarity to previously fielded systems.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-7. New equipment training planning considerations
chunks.text: Current state of the training base to support the equipment.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-7. New equipment training planning considerations
chunks.text:
Impact on training by interim contractor maintenance support and warranty restraints on equipment
and systems.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-7. New equipment training planning considerations
chunks.text: The fielding rate and depot-level maintenance training requirements in support of NET.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-7. New equipment training planning considerations
chunks.text:
The availability of trainers in the gaining units to proliferate training, the quality and quantity of per-
sonnel to be trained, and the effect on unit readiness.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-7. New equipment training planning considerations
chunks.text:
Available training aids, devices, equipment, ranges, facilities, and materials, including visual infor-
mation products.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-7. New equipment training planning considerations
chunks.text:
The environment in which the system is to be employed. This includes and pre-use analysis to iden-
tify safety and occupational health training needs.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-7. New equipment training planning considerations
chunks.text: The capabilities and dispersion of RC units and the affected members of the IRR.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-7. New equipment training planning considerations
chunks.text: The increased costs associated with RC NET due to unit dispersion.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-7. New equipment training planning considerations
chunks.text: Fiscal and manpower resources.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-7. New equipment training planning considerations
chunks.text:
Ammunition and consumables to support NET. This included sufficient lead-time (scheduling ammo)
prior to the conduct of the NET to prevent delays or cancellation of events.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-7. New equipment training planning considerations
chunks.text: Foreign language requirements for foreign national and host nation personnel.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-7. New equipment training planning considerations
chunks.text:
The need to establish project development identifiers and project skill identifiers in accordance with
DA Pam 611-21.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-7. New equipment training planning considerations
chunks.text:
NET live fire events, will be conducted in accordance with AR 5-13, and should include boresight,
zero and qualification of the weapon system as applicable.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-8. Contractual requirements
chunks.text:
Development of NET course materials and the conduct of NET may be accomplished by the Army
or by contractor personnel. When NET strategies incorporate the use of contractors, the NET manager
will serve as the NET Contracting Officer’s Technical Representative. Because of the increased cost fac-
tor, every effort will be made to rapidly transition NET responsibilities from contractor-provided support to
Army-provided support (in accordance with applicable contractual terms or obligations). NET course ma-
terials must be developed in TDC in accordance with TRADOC standards.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-8. Contractual requirements
chunks.text: Respond to data calls from the PEO and PM.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-8. Contractual requirements
chunks.text: Develop the training statement of work in coordination with the TNGDEV.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-8. Contractual requirements
chunks.text: Coordinate contract requirements with the contracting officer/specialist.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-8. Contractual requirements
chunks.text:
Participate in source selection evaluation boards as the subject matter expert for training, as re-
quired.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-8. Contractual requirements
chunks.text:
Conduct an initial training review conference with contractor personnel no later than 30 days after
contract award unless there is a mutual agreement between the NET manager, PEO or PM, and the con-
tractor to reschedule.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-8. Contractual requirements
chunks.text:
If NET is developed and conducted in-house by the Army, MATDEV or provider will train NETT at
the initiation of Defense Acquisition Milestone Decision Review Milestone B.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-9. Developing training support components
chunks.text:
The NET manager coordinates for all training support components to be available to support NET,
to include components required to train the NET team before NET, to train unit operators/maintainers dur-
ing NET, and for unit leaders to conduct sustainment training after NET. To the extent practical, NET and
training support are based on sustainment training strategies developed by the TNGDEV.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-9. Developing training support components
chunks.text: The NET manager will-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-9. Developing training support components
chunks.text:
Evaluate training development and provide a list of required support equipment to the PEO or PM
before Defense Acquisition milestone decision review milestones B and C.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-9. Developing training support components
chunks.text:
In accordance with the approved capabilities document and approved program baseline, MATDEV
in coordination with the proponent and gaining ACOM, ASCC, DRU, will program and budget for develop-
ment of system training support for operators and maintainers.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-10. Conducting new equipment training plan coordination
chunks.text:
The NET Manager organizes the NET team that conducts NET. The NET team is a group of experi-
enced individuals with varying specialties for initial training on the maintenance and operation of new and
modified equipment. A NET team can consist of military, Government, and contractor personnel, or a
combination of each. NETT instructors must meet TRADOC instructor standards. Similarly, the NET team
may use one or a combination of the following techniques:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-10. Conducting new equipment training plan coordination
chunks.text:
Leader training. Training provided to selected unit leaders from the user ACOM, ASCC, or DRU
who, depending upon the complexity and density of equipment, and availability of training facilities, will
train other personnel or units. This training may be conducted at a MATDEV site, or the installation re-
ceiving the equipment.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-10. Conducting new equipment training plan coordination
chunks.text:
Key personnel. Training provided to a select number of key personnel responsible for operating
and maintaining low-density systems. Training and cost effectiveness will dictate the number of locations
where training will be conducted.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-10. Conducting new equipment training plan coordination
chunks.text:
Organizational training. Training provided for personnel assembled for unit training at one location
following advanced individual training. This training can be conducted at a selected station or at the train-
ing base, and thereafter, integrated into unit training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-10. Conducting new equipment training plan coordination
chunks.text:
Unit training. Training provided to all assigned operators and maintainers of a gaining unit for com-
plex equipment that is critical to unit readiness. This is the least desirable strategy because it requires a
large NETT for an extended period.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-10. Conducting new equipment training plan coordination
chunks.text:
Contractor New Equipment Training. Training performed by contractors and managed by the NET
manager. This training may be conducted at a contractor facility, unit location, or at a resident training in-
stallation.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-10. Conducting new equipment training plan coordination
chunks.text:
Institutional training. Training performed by the Army’s institutional training base providing suffi-
cient graduates (both initial entry and first-line supervisor) in time to support the fielding of new equip-
ment.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-10. Conducting new equipment training plan coordination
chunks.text:
Distributed learning. Delivery of training to Soldiers and units through the application of multiple
means and technology. The amount and kind of training appropriate for DL application will be determined
by the tasks to be trained. DL allows students, leaders, and units centralized access to essential infor-
mation and training. It represents a powerful capability in which the proper balance of course content and
delivery technologies are provided when and where they will have the greatest impact on force readiness.
It can include print, web or DVD, or a combination of each. DL should be considered for NET when it is
cost-effective. Exceptions may be required for security reasons or the need to observe hands-on perfor-
mance.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-10. Conducting new equipment training plan coordination
chunks.text:
Full Task Training with training aids, devices, simulators, and simulations. Training provided to in-
dividuals and units using a combination of TADSS (Live, Virtual/Gaming, Constructive (LV/GC) to train
individual and collective tasks in preparation for NET with the operational equipment/ weapon systems.
TADSS includes interactive multimedia instruction courseware, virtual simulators, and constructive simu-
lations. When available, these TADSS should be used to conduct training prior to NET. The overall train-
ing approach is cost-effective and can reduce the time required for NET and permits the gaining unit to
more rapidly transition to a state of operational readiness.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-10. Conducting new equipment training plan coordination
chunks.text:
Embedded training. Training capability hosted in hardware and/or software, integrated into the
overall equipment configuration. Embedded training capabilities will be evaluated and considered as a
preferred means to incorporate training subsystems into the development and follow-on product improve-
ment programs for Army materiel systems. Due to the fact that embedded training affects the overall de-
sign and function of a capability, it must be considered as a KPP or KSA in requirements documents. Em-
bedded training from the requirement document will parallel with definitive embedded training requirement
language transcribed into STRAP.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-10. Conducting new equipment training plan coordination
chunks.text:
Review or update basis of issue plan feeder data, Qualitative & Quantitative Personnel Require-
ments Information data and NET plans, as required.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-10. Conducting new equipment training plan coordination
chunks.text: Review or update program source documents, as required.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-10. Conducting new equipment training plan coordination
chunks.text: Monitor contractual training efforts.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-10. Conducting new equipment training plan coordination
chunks.text:
Oversee and evaluate development of training materials and ensure compatibility with applicable
publications and ensure validation of all System training support products, including updating training ma-
terials in TDC.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-10. Conducting new equipment training plan coordination
chunks.text: Forecast NETT funding requirements in coordination with the PEOs and PMs.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-10. Conducting new equipment training plan coordination
chunks.text: Manage overall NETT requirements.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-10. Conducting new equipment training plan coordination
chunks.text: Determine qualifications for NET course attendance.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-10. Conducting new equipment training plan coordination
chunks.text: Coordinate or monitor conduct of training by NETT members.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-10. Conducting new equipment training plan coordination
chunks.text: Coordinate NET requirements with key players.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-10. Conducting new equipment training plan coordination
chunks.text: The NETT members will-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-10. Conducting new equipment training plan coordination
chunks.text: Conduct NET as prescribed in the NET plan.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-10. Conducting new equipment training plan coordination
chunks.text: Conduct pre-mission and post-mission briefings with the gaining commands.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-10. Conducting new equipment training plan coordination
chunks.text: Set up classrooms and coordinate other required training facilities with the gaining command.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-10. Conducting new equipment training plan coordination
chunks.text: Perform necessary administrative functions.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-10. Conducting new equipment training plan coordination
chunks.text: Assist the gaining command in certification and qualification training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-10. Conducting new equipment training plan coordination
chunks.text: Update training materials as required.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-10. Conducting new equipment training plan coordination
chunks.text:
Ensure operator/maintainer training support and unit employment training support is provided to the
gaining command for sustainment training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-10. Conducting new equipment training plan coordination
chunks.text:
MATDEV will coordinate the funding requirements for conduct of NET with the proponent and gain-
ing ACOM, ASCC, or DRU.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-10. Conducting new equipment training plan coordination
chunks.text:
Systems are considered to have reached full operational capacity (FOC) only after institutional train-
ing organizations have received the total scheduled equipment allocation and completed instructor & key
personnel training (IKPT) to proponent certified instructors to train the necessary replacements to sustain
all units who have completed the NET or DET. Specifics of FOC for a particular system are defined in the
system’s capability documents. In the case of extended NET/DET plans, the institutional training organi-
zation can be incrementally supplied with equipment and personnel to train the necessary replacements
for those units who have already completed the NET/DET; however, incremental fielding to the institu-
tional organization will limit its capability to generate replacements to units who have completed the
NET/DET and delay FOC.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-11. Termination of new equipment training
chunks.text:
The NET manager, in coordination with the PEOs and PMs, CAPDEV, and TNGDEV, will recom-
mend termination of NET when the training milestones and transition to Army training sustainment cen-
ters and institutions are completed.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-11. Termination of new equipment training
chunks.text: The NET manager will initiate the termination of NET for a system as follows (in sequence):

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-11. Termination of new equipment training
chunks.text: Request written concurrence from the appropriate ACOM, ASCC, DRU, DARNG, or CAR.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-11. Termination of new equipment training
chunks.text: Request retirement to DCS, G-3/5/7 (G-3/5/7 TR).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-12. Funding for new equipment training
chunks.text:
The NET manager annually prepares, coordinates, and submits NET program and budget require-
ments to the MATDEV until transition to Army training sustainment centers and institutions is complete.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-12. Funding for new equipment training
chunks.text:
PEOs and PMs provide funding to the NET manager to support new or modified equipment training
requirements. Applicable references include AR 70-1, AR 770-2, and DA Pam 770-2.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-12. Funding for new equipment training
chunks.text:
Paragraph 7-23 provides a list of NET support and services that are reimbursable by the PEOs and
PMs.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-13. New equipment training documentation
chunks.text:
All aspects of NET will be documented in the NET plan, including training support (excludes classi-
fied NET plan).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-13. New equipment training documentation
chunks.text:
The designated NET manager has primary oversight for NETP in coordination with the system PEO
and PM.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-13. New equipment training documentation
chunks.text:
The NET manager, in coordination with the PEOs and PMs, prepares a draft NETP within 30 days of
forwarding the initial Qualitative & Quantitative Personnel Requirements Information to U.S. Army Force
Management Support Activity.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-13. New equipment training documentation
chunks.text: The NET manager disseminates the NET plan for Armywide staffing.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-13. New equipment training documentation
chunks.text:
The NET manager, TNGDEV, CAPDEV, and HRC update designated sections of the NET plan ac-
cordingly.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-13. New equipment training documentation
chunks.text: NET plan changes as equipment, operations, maintenance and fielding concepts are developed.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-13. New equipment training documentation
chunks.text: NET Managers convene training support work group meetings to address NET related issues.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-13. New equipment training documentation
chunks.text:
Coordinate all NETP with DCS, G-3/5/7 (G-3/5/7 TR and DCS, G-8). Approval authority on all
NETP is DCS, G-3/ 5/7.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-14. Agencies requiring new equipment training
chunks.text: A variety of agencies may require training on new equipment, as listed below.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-14. Agencies requiring new equipment training
chunks.text: Plan and coordinate IKPT.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-14. Agencies requiring new equipment training
chunks.text: Monitor the conduct of IKPT.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-14. Agencies requiring new equipment training
chunks.text: Program and budget travel and per diem for NET team personnel to attend IKPT.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-14. Agencies requiring new equipment training
chunks.text: Provide program and budget projections to MATDEV.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-14. Agencies requiring new equipment training
chunks.text:
The Staff Planner’s Orientation Course is conducted for staff personnel who are engaged in plan-
ning, programming, budgeting, and approving programs for the development, production, distribution,
support, and use of new or modified equipment. Normally the orientation is conducted during the research
and development phase of system acquisition. For non-developmental item acquisition, this orientation
occurs during the production phase. The PEO and PM will fund the Staff Planner’s Course. The NET
manager conducts a staff planner’s orientation when warranted by the density, population, and complexity
of the new equipment.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-14. Agencies requiring new equipment training
chunks.text: Provide subject matter experts to conduct the training briefing.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-14. Agencies requiring new equipment training
chunks.text:
Provide budgetary projections and funding requirements to the PEO and PM to support the conduct
of the NMIB.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-14. Agencies requiring new equipment training
chunks.text: Determine NET team composition.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-14. Agencies requiring new equipment training
chunks.text:
Coordinate NET team deployment with the PEOs and PMs, CAPDEV, TNGDEV, and gaining units
as required.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-14. Agencies requiring new equipment training
chunks.text:
Plan and provide program and budget input to MATDEV for NET team requirements. Consider the
Instructor Certification requirements outlined by the TNGDEV in the STRAP.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-14. Agencies requiring new equipment training
chunks.text: Provide training input to MATDEV.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-14. Agencies requiring new equipment training
chunks.text: Plan, coordinate, and provide training (or monitor contractor training) for test support players.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-14. Agencies requiring new equipment training
chunks.text: Review and provide input to the test and evaluation master plan.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-14. Agencies requiring new equipment training
chunks.text: Plan, coordinate, and develop LAR training courses.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-14. Agencies requiring new equipment training
chunks.text: Monitor and conduct LAR training, as applicable.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-14. Agencies requiring new equipment training
chunks.text: Plan, coordinate, and provide program and budget input to the PEO and PM for LAR training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-14. Agencies requiring new equipment training
chunks.text:
Review depot maintenance work request or best commercial practice contractor material for train-
ing impact, as required.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-14. Agencies requiring new equipment training
chunks.text:
Develop, plan, program, coordinate, and monitor depot-level maintenance training, to include train-
ing for test, measurement, and diagnostic equipment.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-14. Agencies requiring new equipment training
chunks.text: Provide program and budget input for depot level training to the PEOs and PMs.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-14. Agencies requiring new equipment training
chunks.text:
Plan, coordinate, and provide program and budget input to the PEOs and PMs for conduct of new
materiel operations and maintenance training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-14. Agencies requiring new equipment training
chunks.text:
Ensure training is provided to a minimum of two NET team members. Training should be provided
after milestone decision review Milestone B, but sufficiently early for NET team to develop a training pro-
gram prior to the start of the staff planners’ course and test support training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-14. Agencies requiring new equipment training
chunks.text: Develop, plan, program, conduct, and evaluate technical orientation training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-14. Agencies requiring new equipment training
chunks.text: Coordinate training requirements with the PEOs and PMs.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-14. Agencies requiring new equipment training
chunks.text: Plan, program, and provide budgetary input to the MATDEVs.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-15. Reserve Component considerations
chunks.text:
New equipment training will consider the unique challenges inherent in modernizing RC units. De-
tailed NET planning is essential between MATDEV (PEOs and PMs and NET managers), and DARNG,
CAR, and the gaining commands to ensure that objectives are met.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-15. Reserve Component considerations
chunks.text: Specific RC NET planning considerations include, but are not limited to, the following:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-15. Reserve Component considerations
chunks.text: Sufficient time to ensure adequate planning.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-15. Reserve Component considerations
chunks.text: Leader training at RC unit locations.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-15. Reserve Component considerations
chunks.text: Sufficient time to program and budget funds to support training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-15. Reserve Component considerations
chunks.text:
Due to the limited number of training days available to RC units, complex systems may require that
NET be extended. A new equipment training strategy that extends beyond two consecutive annual train-
ing periods requires DCS, G-3/5/7 approval. Mobilization personnel are eligible for concurrent training
with the gaining unit.
Section III
Displaced Equipment

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-16. Displaced equipment training determination
chunks.text:
Displaced equipment and software, while not new to the Army, may be new to a receiving unit. As a result
of displaced equipment, training base schools for operators and maintainers have been established. Con-
sequently, units receiving displaced equipment may not need extensive training and may not need exten-
sive formalized planning for that training. This determination will be made by the TNGDEV, identified in
the STRAP, in coordination with the gaining command and the PM of the displaced system.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-17. Reserve Component displaced equipment determination
chunks.text:
Displaced equipment training will consider the unique challenges inherent in resetting RC units. Detailed
DET/NET planning is essential to mitigate effects of displaced equipment between MATDEV (PEOs and
PMs and DET/NET Managers), and DARNG, CAR, and the gaining commands. Due to the limited num-
ber of training days available to RC units, complex systems may require that DET/NET be extended. A
displaced equipment training strategy that extends beyond two consecutive annual training periods re-
quires DCS, G-3/5/7 approval.
Section IV
Doctrine and Tactics Training

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-18. Doctrine and tactics training summary
chunks.text:
Doctrine and tactics training provides guidance to commanders, leaders, staff, and crews/operators on
how to employ the combat capabilities of new or improved materiel or organizations. When required, DTT
is conducted in accordance with the STRAP. When required and feasible, DTT should be conducted prior
to NET/DET. For complex systems requiring NET, DTT may be required to be taught during or directly
after NET.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-19. Doctrine and tactics training policy
chunks.text:
The requirement for DTT will be based on two determinations: Does the new/improved system sig-
nificantly change the unit’s how-to-fight doctrine, and does the unit need help learning how to employ the
new/improved system to accomplish its wartime/design mission?

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-19. Doctrine and tactics training policy
chunks.text:
TNGDEV will identify the requirement for DTT upon receipt of the draft NET plan or DET plan. This
will be accomplished by:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-19. Doctrine and tactics training policy
chunks.text:
Conducting reviews of the applicable operational concepts generated by the requirements determi-
nation process and the organizational and operational plan prepared by CAPDEV for the specific system.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-19. Doctrine and tactics training policy
chunks.text:
Ensuring timely submission to MATDEV of identification of requirements and the concept for DTT
for inclusion in NET plan.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-19. Doctrine and tactics training policy
chunks.text:
If, required, TNGDEV will develop a training strategy to accomplish DTT. In cases where NET may
not require DTT, TNGDEV will ensure that NET plan is annotated to show “DTT not required.”

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-20. Planning for doctrine and tactics training
chunks.text:
Planning will be initiated concurrently with the development of NET plans and/or DET plans. NET
plan or DET plan will include DTT concept and execution for training ACOM, ASCC, and DRU personnel.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-20. Planning for doctrine and tactics training
chunks.text:
DTT planning covers the uses and functions of a new system or organization that must be transmit-
ted to user personnel, so they can fully exploit the new capabilities and improve combat effectiveness.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-20. Planning for doctrine and tactics training
chunks.text: The DTT planning considerations include:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-20. Planning for doctrine and tactics training
chunks.text: Threat.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-20. Planning for doctrine and tactics training
chunks.text: Changes to current doctrine or tactics.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-20. Planning for doctrine and tactics training
chunks.text: Changes in new system or organization.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-20. Planning for doctrine and tactics training
chunks.text: Technical complexity of the new system.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-20. Planning for doctrine and tactics training
chunks.text: Fielding rates.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-20. Planning for doctrine and tactics training
chunks.text: Training strategy for the new system or organization.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-20. Planning for doctrine and tactics training
chunks.text: Planned density for the system or organization.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-20. Planning for doctrine and tactics training
chunks.text: Number of personnel to be trained.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-20. Planning for doctrine and tactics training
chunks.text: Available personnel to provide DTT.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-20. Planning for doctrine and tactics training
chunks.text: Environment and location where DTT will be executed.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-20. Planning for doctrine and tactics training
chunks.text: Capabilities and limitations of units to execute DTT.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-20. Planning for doctrine and tactics training
chunks.text: Funding.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-20. Planning for doctrine and tactics training
chunks.text: Sustainment training following DTT.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-20. Planning for doctrine and tactics training
chunks.text: Software Section V Sustainment Training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-21. Sustainment training coordination
chunks.text:
This section provides policies and planning considerations for the support of sustainment training upon
termination of NET or DET. Sustainment training is a major challenge at all echelons of the Army. The
training community may experience difficulty in sustaining or increasing knowledge gained as a result of
NET, DET, and other Army training programs. The ACOM, ASCC, DRU, CAPDEV, TNGDEV, MATDEV,
and HQDA collectively ensure effective training programs exist or are developed to help sustain the capa-
bility of units to conduct training and will be identified in the STRAP.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-22. Sustainment training policy
chunks.text:
Sustainment training sustains the proficiency of operators, maintainers, and unit leaders to employ the
new/improved system achieved during NET/DET or during Generating Force schools. Accordingly, sus-
tainment training leverages the training support materials used for NET/DET and DTT.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-22. Sustainment training policy
chunks.text:
The MATDEV designs, develops, and provides the training support needed by leaders to sustain
proficiency of system operators, and maintainers. During NET, the MATDEV train leaders on how to use
training support for operators, and maintainers.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-22. Sustainment training policy
chunks.text:
The TNGDEV designs, develops, and provides the training support needed by leaders to sustain
proficiency of leaders on employment, and tactics. During DTT, the TNGDEV trains leaders on how to use
training support for system employment.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-22. Sustainment training policy
chunks.text:
The TNGDEV is responsible to integrate operating/maintaining/employing sustainment into an inte-
grated unit strategy for maneuver training (that is, a CATS for each type of MTOE) and for weapons train-
ing (that is, a weapons training strategy for each type of MTOE).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-23. Sustainment training planning and execution
chunks.text: Automated data processing support. Equipment and maintenance.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-23. Sustainment training planning and execution
chunks.text:
Army modernization training automation system. Contract support, Hardware and software and
Training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-23. Sustainment training planning and execution
chunks.text:
Test support training. Test support packages, follow-on test and evaluation, initial operation test and
evaluation.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-23. Sustainment training planning and execution
chunks.text:
New equipment training related instruction. Instructor and key personnel training, Staff planners
course, NET team instruction, New materiel introductory briefings, Logistics assistance representative
training, New materiel operations and maintenance training, Technical orientation training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-23. Sustainment training planning and execution
chunks.text: Army Civilian support : Salaries, Travel and per diem.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-23. Sustainment training planning and execution
chunks.text: New equipment training team support. Salaries (Army Civilian and contractor), Travel and per diem.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 7 > 7-23. Sustainment training planning and execution
chunks.text:
Contractor support. Salaries, travel, and per diem.
